# Thread: Kidney tumor, recurrent bladder infections
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 47
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-07-26T19:32:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41247>

Hey guys, just wanted to talk this out with ya'all since you guys are like family...

My wife, 55, 5'2, 120lbs. Skinny her whole life. Lots of medical issues through the years. Interstitial Cystitis, Endometriosis, total hysterectomy 6 years ago due to large teratoma, GERD, IBS, lactose intolerant, stomach ulcers a few years ago.

Around December, she got a UTI and they gave her antibiotics. After 6 months of rounds, she is still getting UTI's and bladder infections. At my urging, she got an MRI a couple weeks ago and they found a 1.7cm tumor on her kidney. She's got a surgery date of 15 Sep to get it removed via DaVinci surgery at the Swedish Medical Center in Seattle.

She's not on board with paleo or CT, but since I'm the cook, I make sure she eats pretty good. She has always had a sweet tooth and gets lots of calories from sugar. I can't control everything she eats, but I do dog her a bit hoping she will see the folly of her ways.

I was reading about Apple Cider Vinegar for bladder infections and bought her a bottle yesterday. I think she needs to get over this infection problem before her surgery, but I really don't see how she can. We have bone broth a couple times a week, but she is such a picky eater with her Lactose Intolerance, GERD and IBS that anything out of her comfort zone is hard for her to eat. Most green veggies are out, she hates seafood, she eats lots of chicken, rice, and venison.

It's hard for me to watch this all unfold, because I can't see it ending well. Any thoughts?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-26T19:54:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41261>

Sorry you and your wife are going through this. DH had a very similar perspective on paleo/CT etc, until I sent him to Jack for a 2nd opinion on his back. Lets just say - spending the 30-45min DH spent w/Jack was eye opening for him. he came back insisted we throw away all grain products, and he's on 100% on board w/Epi-Paleo WOL.

If you can swing it - I would recommend you have your wife do an educational consult w/Jack BEFORE the surgery, then do a follow up w/him once you know the status of the tumor.

While its hard to watch your loved one's make decisions that hurt them, they've got to want to make the change. And lets be honest - no spouse is ever good at taking direction on changing their ways from their other half. DH wasn't going to listen to me, wouldn't listen to our Chiropractor, but he did listen to Jack. He thin slices the info - so you understand it, and you can determine what you're going to do to fix it. Understanding what's really at stake, can make you change quite quickly....

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T20:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41280>

I'd say the extended use of abx killed the good flora and now she's vulnerable for recurrent infections.

Probiotics would be my point of attack, obviously, that won't help much if she also eats tons of sugar, now has an even worse leaky gut from the abx.... etc. I think something like this can be the breaking point for some of us. We're "fine" until we do something that entirely throws us off and then our underlying issues start staring us into the face until we can't ignore them any longer.

Cranberry helps with UTI's, too.

### indigogirl — 2012-07-26T21:23:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41307>

Hi Akman,

Sorry your wife and you are going through this.

I had frequent UTI's for years. TMI, but apparently the diaphragm I was using for birth control was not helping. I took one antibiotic (first macrodantin, then switched to Trimethoprim) post coitally for years to prevent infections.

Cranberry juice and D- Mannose did not help me.

Other than being 80% Paleo ( I still have a sugar addiction) and religiously taking probiotics I stopped taking the antibiotics about a month ago and I am not getting UTI's. EDIT: Souldanzer reminded me that in his recent Webinar Jack said fermented veggies (kimchee, sauerkraut, and pickles) are a better way to get probiotics than from a supplement.

Don't know if any of the above will be useful to your wife...

I think Shijin's advice is good, and that a consult with Jack will be helpful.

Best of luck with all this.

Edit...D-Mannose didn't help me, but maybe it will help your wife:

[http://www.d-mannoseworks.com/](http://http://www.d-mannoseworks.com/)

[http://www.squidoo.com/d-mannose](http://http://www.squidoo.com/d-mannose)

Can't find a link...Whole Foods had a D- Mannose powder supplement to take during a UTI...it looked good.

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T22:45:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41328>

I can't say much about the UTI except that cranberry extract helped me the one time I had one.

Hopefully your wife's tumor will be benign.

But....please go get a copy of Suzanne Somers 2008 book "Knockout". You read it immediately, then give it to your wife if needed. She interviews M.D.'s who are using alternative means to cure cancers of all types. **It's a real eye-opener.** Even for people who would never consider alternative treatment there are chapters on how to make conventional treatment more effective and easier to tolerate. There is also great info on prevention.

Actually, I take back the "if needed" above. Everyone needs this book.

### AKMan — 2012-07-26T23:14:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41338>

Thanks, guys. I will order "Knockout" today. Hopefully the kidney tumor isn't cancer. 5 years ago it was 1.3mm, now 1.7cm so they want to take it out no matter what.

I was doing really good working on healing her gut with foods she could eat, bone broth, no artificial sweeteners, and weaning her off sugar...then the uti's hit hard. I think her gut is so leaky from years of poor nutrition and antibiotics that it will take a while, but she goes back to comfort foods when she's not feeling well (cookies, red licorice, cereal, hard candies). I tried sneaking kale in a few weeks ago and she was on toilet all night--she's really sensitive to cruciferous and green leafy veg in general.

She's had several rounds of skin cancer in past 10 years, her father died of lung cancer (smoker) last year, mother is 75 and very poor health (lupus, bronchitis, also smoker). Wife has never smoked, luckily.

Have any of you ever heard of Apple Cider Vinegar for UTI's? Lots of anecdotal evidence on web that it works by changing ph of urine.

> Have any of you ever heard of Apple Cider Vinegar for UTI's? Lots of anecdotal evidence on web that it works by changing ph of urine.

AKMan, I'm really sorry to hear about your wife's struggles. +1000 on getting Knock Out. You don't have to have cancer to benefit from this book.... and it's very empowering when dealing with the medical system because you can a) support yourself and b) know what good care looks like. (You definitely need to prepare the body for surgery and surgery-recovery, too, even if it isn't cancer... and one of the last chapters talks about supporting the body when choosing conventional means, too, so something for everyone.)

ACV is great stuff and I'm sure it's helpful. As are probiotics, fermented veggies, etc. The thing is, once you get to the point she is at (and I can relate, even though my issues presented differently), the small helpful things that heal others won't get you there. I pretty much had to eliminate all green veggies and cruciferous ones for a long while. I concentrated on good fat and good protein, now with a focus on seafood.

Has she had any blood testing? A basic work up (and a consult with Dr K) would likely be a huge wake up call.

In my case, getting sick was exactly the wake up call I needed. When you can 'manage' and get by you don't tend to do the radical shifts you need to really address the problems. I hope this illness can be what she needs to light the proverbial fire. Especially as she's already had skin cancer.

Good luck to you both!

### Joann — 2012-07-26T23:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41355>

Akman, this sounds so familiar to me what you are saying about your wife.

I am assuming she does not use hormones. A hysterectomy = no estradiol which is needed in bladder area ....without enough estrogen the urethra gets too thin and can cause uti.

estriol is a weak estrogen but works well.

Susanne Somers definitely uses BHRT, just saying

### AKMan — 2012-07-27T00:16:01+0300

She has been on Estratest since her hysterectomy. She also takes Prilosec for GERD. Other than that 5000IU/day Vit D3 and two or three different of probiotic formulas. She had a really bad case of wrist tendonitis and did two rounds of cortisone shots in the past year, wonder if there's a connection since that's about when the UTIs started showing up...

She was in the hospital getting a cystoscopy a couple weeks ago to check for bladder cancer (all clear), they started an IV of Cipro antibiotics and as soon as it hit her vein, she started breaking out in big red welts up her arm. They stopped that quickly and used a different antibiotic, not sure what, but ever since when she takes the antibiotics they gave her for UTI, she gets hives all over and must take Benedryl along with it.

Thanks for talking this out with me, she is under doctor care and has tons of labwork, maybe someday when this is all under control (or not) we can set something up with Jack.

### AKMan — 2012-07-27T00:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41367>

> **indigogirl;41583 wrote:** Akman,
>
> Please take this with a grain of salt as only your wife and you know what is best for her...
>
>
>
> If you can afford it the time to talk to Jack is now, before her surgery...not "when this is all under control". You need him now to get things under control!
>
>
>
> Also is Estratest a bioidentical hormone? There is a huge difference between synthetic and bioidentical hormones.

According to their website, Estratest is composed of:

Esterified Estrogens

Esterified Estrogens, USP is a mixture of the sodium salts of the sulfate esters of the estrogenic substances, principally estrone, that are of the type excreted by pregnant mares. Esterified Estrogens contain not less than 75.0 percent and not more than 85.0 percent of sodium estrone sulfate, and not less than 6.0 percent and not more than 15.0 percent of sodium equilin sulfate, in such proportion that the total of these two components is not less than 90.0 percent.

Methyltestosterone

Methyltestosterone, USP is an androgen. Androgens are derivatives of cyclopentano-perhydrophenanthrene. Endogenous androgens are C-19 steroids with a side chain at C-17, and with two angular methyl groups. Testosterone is the primary endogenous androgen. Fluoxymesterone and methyltestosterone are synthetic derivatives of testosterone.

So can anyone tell me if it's considered real or synthetic?

As to getting her to talk to Jack, she has no clue who Jack is other than me saying, "I'm in a tub of ice because Jack says it good", just kidding, kinda, but she isn't a blog-reading, web crawler like us--she works with her doctors, does about half of what they say and chalks the rest up to genetics. She's a big girl and uses the system like 98% of other Americans. I have heard Jack say he gives advice and only a portion take it at first, later they come crawling back and are more open to his advice.

AKMan, I know it's been said repeatedly, but I want to suggest again a Skype consult with Dr K. Get whatever labs she has, send them to Dr K, and book a consult... before her surgery. First off, he IS a doctor and she listens to doctors. (His is not only a neurosurgeon, but has been treating people and consulting with people along his paradigm for years now.) Secondly, as someone who has spent a lot of time in front of doctors lately, she will have an amazing experience listening to Dr K. He really speaks to you, in a synthesized way, with YOUR information in mind. He doesn't just give general recommendations for 'conditions like this' he talks about YOU and your specific information. It is totally rare to have someone pay that close attention to you medically... and it is powerful. So even when he gives you the proverbial kick in the pants, it has meaning that other doctors don't get close to.

I totally know what you mean about opening up personally. I feel that way, too. But I found with my consult (and on the forum) that the most sensitive details of my life were not only handled skillfully, but in ways that OPENED up doors for me to do something real and dramatic about it - for the better. She does not have to be a blog reader or forum-poster to get the full benefit. And you will also get the full benefit of the big picture too. Which will give you good information and also help you determine how committed your wife is going to be at this point.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-27T02:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41412>

PS, some research that ties Dr K's work to your wife's (and all of us like her!) desire to eat comfort foods when she doesn't feel well....

**Leptin concentrations in response to acute stress predict subsequent intake of comfort foods**

_Physiology and Behaviour, 2012_

Both animals and humans show a tendency toward eating more “comfort food” (high fat, sweet food) after

acute stress. Such stress eating may be contributing to the obesity epidemic, and it is important to understand

the underlying psychobiological mechanisms. Prior investigations have studied what makes individuals eat

more after stress; this study investigates what might make individuals eat less. Leptin has been shown to

increase following a laboratory stressor, and is known to regulate satiety. This study examined whether

leptin reactivity accounts for individual differences in stress eating. To test this, we exposed forty women

to standardized acute psychological laboratory stress (Trier Social Stress Test) while blood was sampled

repeatedly for measurements of plasma leptin. We then measured food intake after the stressor. Increasing

leptin during the stressor predicted lower intake of comfort food. These initial ﬁndings suggest that acute

changes in leptin may be one of the factors modulating down the consumption of comfort food following

stress.

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T18:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41807>

[http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/875.html](http://http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/875.html)

> Improving recovery after surgery. Taking L-arginine with ribonucleic acid (RNA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) before surgery or afterwards seems to help reduce the recovery time, reduce the number of infections, and improve wound healing after surgery.
> The following doses have been studied in scientific research:
>
>
>
> BY MOUTH:
>
>
>
> For congestive heart failure: doses range from 6-20 grams per day, as three divided doses.
>
> For chest pain associated with coronary artery disease (angina pectoris): 3-6 grams three times per day for up to one month.
>
> For preventing the loss of the effectiveness of nitroglycerin in relieving pain in people with chest pain due to coronary artery disease (angina pectoris): 700 mg four times daily.
>
> For organic erectile dysfunction (ED): 5 grams per day. Taking lower doses might not be effective.
>
> For preventing inflammation of the digestive tract in premature infants: 261 mg/kg added to oral feedings daily for the first 28 days of life.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-29T18:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-41811>

AKMan, I used to get UTI's all the time, starting in my early 20's. So often that I'd just call my doctor and they'd write Rx for anti-b's over the phone.

Cranberry, apple cider, washing thoroughly, etc....zero help.

So bad that I carried AZO Standard (pain relief for UTI's) in my purse constantly in case I got a UTI before I could get anti-b's.

I know your wife doesn't want to hear it, but what stopped them (and I haven't had one now in over 2 years)...Paleo diet. Cutting out sugar & grain.

Your wife saying because she's thin she can eat whatever she wants is the problem. As Dr. K. says, looks like a rockstar on the outside, but a mess on the inside.

If she can't see that sugar & grain are the source, and the anti-b's have basically ruined her gut flora and are keeping the problem going, then you have to find a way to get her to see this.

Leave her because she won't eat epi-Paleo? lol, of course not. But you care about her and don't want to see her further down the hole.

Shoot, I just had this huge talk with my parents about all this stuff, but they say "We're in our late 70's, what's a little pasta going to hurt us now?" So I hear you.

Gave my mom a bag of Organic unsweetened coconut and told her to eat a spoonful of it with every meal. Guess what she tells me last night..."Oh I love the coconut! I put it on my cereal, on my fruit, in my ice cream". Sheesh.

Even seeing me go from 250 lbs on a sackful of medicines to 165 and no meds, she agrees what I'm doing is working, she's just not ready to give up grains and sugar. I feel for her, though, because she is so limited in what she can eat with her digestive problems and bladder issues.

On the plus side, I was out splitting firewood and my neighbor came tromping through the woods with a massive slab of King Salmon he just caught, so my epi-paleo dinner is taken care of for a few days!

### AKMan — 2012-09-20T19:33:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-tumor-recurrent-bladder-infections.2697/post-52938>

> **AKMan;41464 wrote:** Hey guys, just wanted to talk this out with ya'all since you guys are like family...
>
>
>
> My wife, 55, 5'2, 120lbs. Skinny her whole life. Lots of medical issues through the years. Interstitial Cystitis, Endometriosis, total hysterectomy 6 years ago due to large teratoma, GERD, IBS, lactose intolerant, stomach ulcers a few years ago.
>
>
>
> Around December, she got a UTI and they gave her antibiotics. After 6 months of rounds, she is still getting UTI's and bladder infections. At my urging, she got an MRI a couple weeks ago and they found a 1.7cm tumor on her kidney. She's got a surgery date of 15 Sep to get it removed via DaVinci surgery at the Swedish Medical Center in Seattle.
>
>
>
> She's not on board with paleo or CT, but since I'm the cook, I make sure she eats pretty good. She has always had a sweet tooth and gets lots of calories from sugar. I can't control everything she eats, but I do dog her a bit hoping she will see the folly of her ways.
>
>
>
> I was reading about Apple Cider Vinegar for bladder infections and bought her a bottle yesterday. I think she needs to get over this infection problem before her surgery, but I really don't see how she can. We have bone broth a couple times a week, but she is such a picky eater with her Lactose Intolerance, GERD and IBS that anything out of her comfort zone is hard for her to eat. Most green veggies are out, she hates seafood, she eats lots of chicken, rice, and venison.
>
>
>
> It's hard for me to watch this all unfold, because I can't see it ending well. Any thoughts?

Well, we just got back from Seattle. Her surgery went well, they removed the tumour from her kidney and found it to be a non-cancerous oncocytoma. So now several weeks of down-time for her with lots of bone broth and as much seafood as I can get her to eat, which won't be much.

It was an incredible experience seeing a huge medical complex like the Swedish Center in Seattle. It's in an area called First Hill in downtown Seattle, block after block of high-rise hospitals, cancer centers, transplant centers..amazing, and boggles the mind to think how much money goes through an area like this. Hospital food was pathetic, sleep impossible for recovering surgery patients.

Anyway, just wanted to follow up on wife's status.

### lee — 2012-07-28T12:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-sequenced-posts-have-links-to-prior-posts-in-the-series-ex-cpc-5.2714/post-41648>

Thanks all of you! I really appreciate your taking the time to explain this. I just found the site yesterday and kept landing on amazing articles that were #3 or #5. I'm addicted now, and hope I don't get resistant from overloading. I've foresaken most of the day for your site, it's so good.

I work in the web industry, and newbie first impressions are kind of precious to our company because once we've learned a site, we're less able to provide usability insight. We call it the curse of knowledge, and try to pay it forward when we land on a site we like and are new and can see how someone can save users a few clicks here and there. (Others do the same for us and we've benefitted greatly from their suggestions.) I mean no disrespect.

There's are several plugins for Wordpress for related content that might provide user experience benefit. Related content could show up in a sidebar. Might be helpful, or at least a user interface enhancement since there are so many blog sequences in this extraordinary site.

The Wordpress plugin directory has 2 pages of plugins for related content:

[http://wordpress.org/extend/plugins/tags/related-content](http://http://wordpress.org/extend/plugins/tags/related-content)

Perhaps a future version of the site could use a feature like this. FWIW, it's probably very easy to install.

Love the quilt!

Thanks everyone. I'll use what you showed me.

### Petra — 2012-07-27T12:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41474>

For 2 weeks I'm into to epi paleo fish eating mode. I feel great. But every night I feel really tired. In the afternoon I get foggy in my head. And I'm noticing my focus is shifting more often.

Are these Epi-Paleo detox symptoms ?

Anybody else experiencing this?

Please share,

Petra

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-27T13:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41479>

there are no detox symptoms from fish. If you get tired then it could be because hormones are not optimal.

### Petra — 2012-07-27T14:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41490>

Thanks for your reply Jack. Most appreciated.

Back to the lab it is for me.

Leptin and Cortisol, melatonin seem ok, maybe the estrogen came to play.

Take care,

Petra

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-27T20:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-41545>

> **Jack Kruse;41700 wrote:** there are no detox symptoms from fish. If you get tired then it could be because hormones are not optimal.

well something is happening. And not from tons of seafood b/c I've been doing that for months now. It's from switching to \*raw\* seafood.

My digestive system has decided to completely stop working. I had to up my mg to ridiculous levels that I could never have tolerated before. I get the worst cravings. For the weirdest things (like fish heads.....). And no, I don't like the taste. But I want it anyways. And then nothing will move out of my stomach. It just sits there forever. My stomach is so bloated after a meal that I can't fit into any pants without spanx. I get nauseous after meals, partially b/c I feel too full but even if I eat less I still feel sick to the point of throwing up a few times by now. NOT self-induced, as that might need to be declared in my case  Sometimes I feel sick right away, sometimes up to 2hrs later. This isn't food poisoning either. It goes away a few hours after a meal. I eat too much and I'm constantly hungry. Basically, I feel like I'm back to my first year or so of re-feeding. I feel like cr\*p.

And yes, I'm tired. So tired I am spending half my day in bed. But then I also upped my Lugol's to 150mg/day b/c my fibrocystic symptoms are out of control and I'm scared.

I don't doubt I'm a hormonal mess right now. And maybe it's not detox. But there's \*something\* happening. I guess I could just go back to cooking all my food but that's kinda not my style  And I used to be all into kirlian images as a raw foodie......

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-31T23:42:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42135>

> **Jack Kruse;42374 wrote:** yep......google ADH.

wow.... most interesting. There's my answer to why I'm drinking so much Dr Cardiologist and maybe it's not all mental. I can go way beyond 4L/day if I allow myself to.... this has nothing to do with AN behaviors/hunger suppression me thinks.

So many severe AN's have issues with bladder control/frequent urination. So many of them drink excessive amounts. The standard physician answer is that at a certain level of emaciation the bladder muscle will atrophy and that's why we have to pee every 5min. My level of dehydration from chronic purging limited this effect somewhat during my own AN but boy, once I was re-feeding.... I couldn't go too far from access to bathroom. I'd have some fluids and it would go right through me. It got a bit better with continued recovery but I'm still the one person who needs to pee first out of a group. Constantly thirsty. I suspected diabetes at some point.

Still studying up on it....haven't quite found how it fits with the other hormones. I think someone said it's part of the HPA axis somewhere... at the bottom?

Thanks  Besides the ugh, gotta go back to my PCP it looks like....

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-01T06:12:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42215>

I thought this was pretty cool

> Vasopressin released within the brain has many actions:
>
> It has been implicated in **memory formation, including delayed reflexes, image, short- and long-term memory, though the mechanism remains unknown; these findings are controversial. However, the synthetic vasopressin analogue desmopressin has come to interest as a likely **nootropic**.
>
> Vasopressin is released into the brain in a circadian rhythm by neurons of the supraoptic nucleus.**
>
> Vasopressin released from centrally projecting hypothalamic neurons is involved in aggression, blood pressure regulation and temperature regulation.
>
> Selective AVPr1a blockade in the ventral pallidum has been shown to prevent partner preference, suggesting that these receptors in this ventral forebrain region are crucial for pair bonding.\[3\]
>
> Recent evidence suggests that vasopressin may have analgesic effects. The analgesia effects of vasopressin were found to be dependent on both stress and gender.\[5\]

For the weirdest things (like fish heads.....). And no, I don't like the taste. But I want it anyways. And then nothing will move out of my stomach. It just sits there forever. I get nauseous after meals, partially b/c I feel too full but even if I eat less I still feel sick to the point of throwing up a few times by now. NOT self-induced, as that might need to be declared in my case  Sometimes I feel sick right away, sometimes up to 2hrs later. This isn't food poisoning either. It goes away a few hours after a meal. I eat too much and I'm constantly hungry. Basically, I feel like I'm back to my first year or so of re-feeding. I feel like cr\*p.

**

Hi Souldanzer,

When you say: _"And then nothing will move out of my stomach."_ I don't know your whole story but this symptom describes a common problem that is seen in some people at the **Weston A Price Foundation** that go from high carbohydrate to high protein. Of course, Sally Fallon would say don't go high protein, actually its dangerous, go high fat.

Some people cannot digest protein because of the lack of HCL in the stomach. If this is your problem your digestion will never work properly. You can easily test at home to see if this is your problem. Here is an WAPF essay to read and a betaine HCL home test called the Heidelberg Capsule Test**:

[http://www.modernherbalist.com/betaine.html](http://http://www.modernherbalist.com/betaine.html)

[http://www.westonaprice.org/digestive-disorders/acid-reflux-a-red-flag](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/digestive-disorders/acid-reflux-a-red-flag)

If I were you, during the day, I would get out of bed and slowly walk around outside. Get some sun. After a short walk sit in the sun with your eyes closed and let the light filter through your eye lids. Do as much as you can. Think good thoughts; do not let poisonous thoughts enter your mind. Sleep in as dark as possible during the night. Try not to sleep during the day.

If you are not digesting your food properly, you are doing yourself harm every time you eat.

>
> If I were you, during the day, I would get out of bed and slowly walk around outside. Get some sun. After a short walk sit in the sun with your eyes closed and let the light filter through your eye lids. Do as much as you can. Think good thoughts; do not let poisonous thoughts enter your mind. Sleep in as dark as possible during the night. Try not to sleep during the day.
>
>
>
> If you are not digesting your food properly, you are doing yourself harm every time you eat.

Thanks, Caroline

I'm actually feeling better by the day  But I appreciate your input! I've tried HCL and I can't tolerate it, not b/c of the acid but b/c of what is added to the capsules as a filler.... allergic .... can't take Betaine HCL without capsule and filler so that's a nogo for me. I'm unfortunately very familiar with delayed stomach emptying b/c I'm recovering from anorexia. I"ve been doing pretty well with it though. I think my gut has adjusted except for that I still need a little more mg than before to keep things moving. I don't monitor my food, I eat intuitively, but there definitely is a lot of fat involved!!

I hear you on trying not to stay in bed. There's some days I literally cannot get out of bed (or off the couch) for most of the day. I have really bad headaches from a head injury and probably a congenital heart defect, I just can't do it. I need to be horizontal, quiet, I do try to not make it dark.... but boy some days just getting up to make my food appears like a major exertion. I've been able to go out a bit more again lately, so I think that's helping. I tend to "wake up" when the sun hits my eyes and I have a bit more energy then. I also go to the pool and rest there in the grass or on a lounge chair.

I still have a problem with my stomach and brain not agreeing on food intake. Brain asks for more than stomach can handle. But that's been ongoing for a long time now.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T18:33:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42844>

Souldanzer, I had the exact same problem when I increased my raw fish intake. Increase your water/green tea consumption. This seems to be what did the trick for me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T18:52:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42856>

> **shilohman;43123 wrote:** Souldanzer, I had the exact same problem when I increased my raw fish intake. Increase your water/green tea consumption. This seems to be what did the trick for me.

Thanks, Shiloman. It appears this is rather common so I'm not worried at all anymore.

I'm at 4+ liters/day most of it green tea (decaf).... not counting water in my food. I'm spending half my day peeing, I'm not kidding either. I've been trying to reduce my fluid intake for years now b/c my AN docs say it's an eating disorder behavior but I threw that out the window after Dr K clued me in on what might be really going on (low ADH). I'm thinking of getting it tested via my PCP soon. The one who complains about me drinking too much.... lol. I guess my question isn't so much if that's my problem but what do you do about low ADH?? (yes, other than getting your PFO fixed..... )

The digestion problems have been sorted except for the mg but even that's come down a bit now... I'm taking around 1200mg including 600mg citrate, 400mg chloride, and 2000mg L-threonate. I'll continue to keep dropping it as my gut adjusts.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T19:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42864>

>
>

I've been trying to reduce my fluid intake for years now b/c my AN docs say it's an eating disorder behavior but I threw that out the window after Dr K clued me in on what might be really going on (low ADH). I'm thinking of getting it tested via my PCP soon. The one who complains about me drinking too much.... lol. I guess my question isn't so much if that's my problem but what do you do about low ADH?? (yes, other than getting your PFO fixed..... )
>
>
>
> The digestion problems have been sorted except for the mg but even that's come down a bit now... I'm taking around 1200mg including 600mg citrate, 400mg chloride, and 2000mg L-threonate. I'll continue to keep dropping it as my gut adjusts.

That's a lot of water/tea. So what is ADH?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T19:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42865>

Anti Diuretic hormone. I can't quite fit it in with the other hormones.... comes from your pituitary if I remember correctly. It regulates your water retention/excretion. Too little and you just keep peeing... and therefore get thirsty. A lot.

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T19:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42867>

I found it in "CPC #4 EVOLUTIONARY FRIEND OR FOE", hormone vassopressin (ADH) from the posterior pituitary

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T19:24:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42868>

> **hebsie;43138 wrote:** ...it would be worth investigating/researching diabetes insipidus -- I'm guessing neurogenic DI. Perhaps a trial of desmopressin might be in order.
>
>
>
> What do you say Dr. K??...hebs

Was reading about diabetes insipidus after Dr k's comment. Does sound a lot like me. It gets better at times though. Some days I don't have to pee every 15 min... only every hour or so... lol. My family wouldn't take me on vacation once b/c I needed to pee so often and drink all the time. Got blamed for that one. Just stop doing it, Soul. It's all mental. Right.

I think it also co-incides with chronic brain injuries. And probably PFOs... gah. Double whammy. And lots of my AN/BN friends struggle with this.... another score  In those cases it always gets blamed on the person, though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T20:08:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42879>

Finding that low ADH can either be caused by damage to the hypothalamus, or pituitary, or the kidneys.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T20:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-42883>

> **hebsie;43162 wrote:** ...FWIW, I've had it thrown around in many doctor's appts, diabetes insipidus that is. Testing wise, for me they did a 24 hour urine osmolality (it should average between 500 and 800 mOsm/kg), and then ran a (in my case botched) [water deprivation test](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fluid_deprivation_test) (where they inject you with DDAVP)...hebs

water deprivation test...... that sounds horrific to me....  My cards tested my electrolytes with osmolality (but that was blood?) and it was fine..... but that was after overnight fasting and I have to stop drinking a few hours before bed or I have to pee all night long. And I tend to restrict my fluid intake when there is testing coming up.... b/c I know I drink excessively. Water, that is  And stop drinking a while before I go anywhere, really.... lol. Unless there's bathrooms around.

Well, considering how many people with EDs I know that have this issue.... not that surprising really, hebs. it's just kinda cruel to think about all the IP/IOP places that put restrictions on your fluid intake  b/c it's considered an ED behavior. I highly doubt they look for low ADH before they put people through that. They never tested for me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-24T07:22:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53757>

> **Souldanzer;42352 wrote:** Happy to report that my bloated belly is now shrinking a little every day My mag needs have been cut back in half, which is where I started out before adding raw seafood and my digestion is normalizing. Food actually moves through my gut again. I did cut back the raw for a couple of days, now I'm at about 50% raw of the seafood I eat (I eat mostly only seafood, very little meat).
>
>
>
> The constant need to pee has lessened as well.... wow, that sudden change put my gut and hormones through the ringer! I'm also taking some anti-parasitic herbs still.

Reviving this thread. After listening to tonight's webinar I realized that the mag thing is starting to happen to me. I had to take close to 2000mg just to get things moving after switching to raw epi paleo. Since then, my needs have slowly been dropping. The last few days, I'm not able to tolerate even 300mg of mg anymore..... so I"m assuming this is a good thing? It means I cleared out my biofilms and can start rebuilding?

I unintentionally induced the 2-3 days of number 6/7 a few times lately b/c my mg needs are dropping and I don't realize until I already got the runs

Will drop my dose to 200mg tomorrow

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T15:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53769>

Does anyone else wonder if nightmares might be associated with detoxing? I had a string of AWFUL dreams last night - three in a row, all hitting very close to home, all very disturbing. I woke up from the last one feeling so distraught that I had to sit there in bed convincing myself that dreams are just dreams, it didn't actually happen, etc.

Whew. Hoping that doesn't become a regular feature...

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-24T15:21:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53770>

>
>
>

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T15:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53771>

> **Shijin13;54429 wrote:** Quelsen has a discussion about this in his journal... your mind is letting go of things ... go with it.

Oh, great...so I'm in for a bumpy ride...tray-tables up!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-24T15:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53773>

I've stopped having my reoccurring dream where I'm stuck in a gold fish bowl being chased by a giant goldfish as I try to swim down to the castle to get air.... so that's a win.

its been a long while since I've had really bad dreams.... I think I kicked those out a long time ago... think of all dreams as brain re-wiring. good/bad/indifferent - they're your brains way of dealing w/things it can't figure out in a conscious state... think of it as its your brain reversing ROS w/in its own memory cells.....

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T18:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53814>

I always think that dreams gone bad have something to do with hypoglycemia at night, followed by a release of cortisol/adrenaline to bring up the blood sugar. At least that's what seems to be the pre-curser for mine.

The other night I pulled my dead daughter out of the bottom of a (cloudy) swimming pool, where I had been watching all the kids swimming. That one still shakes me to the bone. It was the daughter I sent to first grade on minimal rations a few days prior, because she refused to help make her lunch!!

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T18:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53817>

> **bigknitwit;54474 wrote:** I always think that dreams gone bad have something to do with hypoglycemia at night, followed by a release of cortisol/adrenaline to bring up the blood sugar. At least that's what seems to be the pre-curser for mine.
>
>
>
> The other night I pulled my dead daughter out of the bottom of a (cloudy) swimming pool, where I had been watching all the kids swimming. That one still shakes me to the bone. It was the daughter I sent to first grade on minimal rations a few days prior, because she refused to help make her lunch!!

In my case, hypoglycemia shouldn't be an issue...but that certainly sounds like a legitimate explanation for some.

In one of my dreams, my beloved Nana had dropped dead out of nowhere (which, given I was reading Destiny's thread on here about her friend dropping dead, I can kind of see where that one came from in retrospect). The other two, though...were just bizarre and violent and unsettling. People very close to me trying to hurt me in sick ways, people who have been in the background of my life since childhood (in real life), chasing me through a dream, trying to hurt me and a friend, and me being ultra-violent fighting him off. Total unadulterated weirdness.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T20:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53862>

> **ealachan;54477 wrote:** In my case, hypoglycemia shouldn't be an issue...but that certainly sounds like a legitimate explanation for some.
>
>
>
> In one of my dreams, my beloved Nana had dropped dead out of nowhere (which, given I was reading Destiny's thread on here about her friend dropping dead, I can kind of see where that one came from in retrospect). The other two, though...were just bizarre and violent and unsettling. People very close to me trying to hurt me in sick ways, people who have been in the background of my life since childhood (in real life), chasing me through a dream, trying to hurt me and a friend, and me being ultra-violent fighting him off. Total unadulterated weirdness.

I used to get really weird, horrendous dreams like that when I was getting off (or on) zoloft. So changes in neurochemistry were a real trigger here. They are very, very unsettling, I remember some of mine, and they were much like yours. I wonder if in your case it's a good sign that you're upregulating some of your neurotransmitters..? I really don't know. I didn't like pulling my dead 6 year old out of the pool though. Still takes my breath away.

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T21:00:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-detox-symptoms.2716/post-53872>

Yeah can't say as I blame you there. The idea of that makes me shiver, and I don't even have kids.

### MartiD — 2012-07-27T16:48:19+0300

### ealachan — 2012-07-27T17:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41526>

Dr. K, are dried sea veg ok? The Asian market near me has several of the varieties you listed, but they're all dried. Most of what I found on Amazon appeared to be dried as well.

### Melinda — 2012-07-27T18:51:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41534>

> **MartiD;41745 wrote:** I've never heard of most of the sea veggies. I have two natural food grocery stores but wonder about access to some of these items. Do I find them in fresh veggie section or are they frozen, canned, or other method? Sorry if this is a dumb question but we don't have much ocean water food options in Mid Missouri!

Another word would be seaweed. I buy them dried at the Asian market. Lots of different choices. I'm going to take the list with me today and try to get a small bag of each. Usually you soak them in water and they grow back to their original size. So it's easy to crumble the dried seaweed into soups or sauces and they get soft or leave them crunchy in salads.

### janagram — 2012-07-27T19:41:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41542>

Dr. K., Thank you for this blog!!!

It confirms what I have been suspecting for awhile....not that I love this prescription...it flies in the face of how I like to eat, but it rings true! I will, forthwith, begin to make changes in line with this information!

### tweety — 2012-07-27T23:51:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41589>

I have found almost all these products at WFM. They all ate tasty and easy to eat! I am excited to see them on the list.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-28T02:09:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41601>

> **ealachan;41747 wrote:** Dr. K, are dried sea veg ok? The Asian market near me has several of the varieties you listed, but they're all dried. Most of what I found on Amazon appeared to be dried as well.

Most often, seaweeds/sea veggies are sold dried. They then rehydrate for cooking/eating. You can use them for seaweed salads or in broths like Dr K said, etc. Dried is much smaller and lighter because all the sea water is evaporated. Just be careful about anything added to them... you want just dried nothing extra.

### Melinda — 2012-07-28T04:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-nutrition-begins-here.2723/post-41622>

# Thread: Eating beans for cultural reasons?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-07-28T04:33:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41624>

At first I couldn't wrap my head around that statement in the latest blog, but then remembered that mom used to make black-eyed peas for New Years. We also had pickled herring and shrimp, and it was the few times a year

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-28T04:37:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41626>

I just made my first batch of homemade pickled herring  I haven't gotten the courage to try some but might tomorrow.

### Michael — 2012-07-28T08:51:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41642>

Yeah. I guess he just meant that some people might want to eat specific dishes that are traditional in their society. There are a lot of "double starch" meals in some parts of the world where good protein sources are less easily available and more costly. Having the two together means you get a full complement of the amino-acids. Nutritionally inferior but better than nothing. For an example, look up "three sisters" on Wikipedia.

Nowadays people might continue to eat those dishes even though they can afford not to _because_ they're a part of their national cuisine, and they have fond memories of their mother's cooking, and so on.

Jack's saying just don't. Not fair on your GI tract!

### kathylu — 2012-07-28T13:12:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41649>

I think Jack was referring to the philosophy of the Weston A. Price folk (WAPF). They say that grains and legumes are perfectly digestible, healthy foods as long as they are prepared in a traditional manner, such as soaking, sprouting and fermenting before being cooked. They also think that almost all people can and should be eating raw dairy, and that raw dairy has quite a few "magical" medical properties (my words).

### Michael — 2012-07-28T14:37:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41652>

Dunno why he would say "cultural" and not "WAPF" then. But all the same to me. .

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-28T14:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41653>

> **kathylu;41873 wrote:** I think Jack was referring to the philosophy of the Weston A. Price folk (WAPF). They say that grains and legumes are perfectly digestible, healthy foods as long as they are prepared in a traditional manner, such as soaking, sprouting and fermenting before being cooked. They also think that almost all people can and should be eating raw dairy, and that raw dairy has quite a few "magical" medical properties (my words).

BINGO WAPF is not correct in my view either......just because we can do something does not mean we should.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-28T15:08:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41657>

Oh, I know exactly what he meant. No more refried beans for me!  No more hummus.

Well, I've actually been avoiding them for several months now.

At least they aren't part of my culture, and I won't upset my parents by not eating them.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-28T16:58:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-beans-for-cultural-reasons.2728/post-41664>

Someone explain this to my mother  I'm German..... it's unacceptable to refuse the bread and cheese, and skipping the potatoes is a federal offense.

She keeps telling me I need my carbohydrates for energy and blames it on AN.... while I drink down a can of coconut milk. Lol.

### Michael — 2012-07-29T20:43:48+0300

> **bigknitwit;42053 wrote:** I'm not sure about sauteeing in macadamia nut oil, olive oil, or avocado oil. Wouldn't it oxidize them?

I don't think olive oiive oil is horrible in that regard. Obviously, you should fry gently and not get anywhere near the smoke point nor re-use the oil. But, as far as i know, it has been used for frying in Southern Europe for a pretty long time.

But I guess you could steam the food and add the olive oil after it was cooked.

### kathylu — 2012-08-05T04:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mufas.2738/post-42999>

> **Cavemam;43273 wrote:** Did we get a yes on using Mac oil for cooking fish?

Unfortunately, it's not a good idea to heat either MUFA's or PUFA's. The double bonds are damaged by heat and the oil degrades, can become rancid. Pour your (expensive) MUFA's cold over baked or grilled fish!

### Melinda — 2012-08-13T00:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mufas.2738/post-44590>

Tallow: I put about 5 lbs of beef fat in my crockpot on high>low. I do this outside in the backyard by the grill to keep the smell out of the house. I let it run all day. If there is still too much unmelted fat after 13-14 hours, I take it in and restart it the next morning. About half way through the second day I'm tired of thinking about it and turn if off and let it cool for about 30 minutes. I lift out the "cracklin's" with a slotted spoon and slowly pour the clear fat into a jellyroll pan, not every last drop as I don't want the dark bits. Let it cool on the counter then cover with foil and refrig overnight. Next day I turn it out onto the foil top and break it into ~1-2" squares, it is snow white. I put all in a bag and freeze. This lasts me about 6 months. It adds wonderful flavor to meat. I wouldn't use it with fish or chicken (I don't eat chicken anymore).

# Thread: So I'm thinking about going over to the dark side

### chocolate — 2012-07-30T19:35:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-41951>

I have a bloated gut and all of the symptoms of proteinuria. I have ridiculous triglycerides last time I checked. My paternal grandpa died of nephritus and my aunt that has been married 6 times had it, but it went away with no protein. She is 79 and just had a pace maker put in. I have a funny feeling it had to do with magnesium levels.

After reading for the past few days... I know what triggered this particular event. It was a day with not enough clean fat, too many nuts with too many minerals, fat and probably too much protein... not feeling so great... I just drank water the next day, ran out in a traffic jam, and binged on cherries that were supposed to be for the whole family. The boobs puffed up... argh.. I just tried to fast it away and thought it dodged a bullet.

Then I ate out and rashed, and my tummy felt like it had been kicked by a mule.

So after reading and reading... I am sure this comes and goes... soy and flaxseed help nephritis. Well, you can't quit dumping calcium until your magnesium is up.... which won't happen unless your estrogen is up. My estrogen went up from the stupid cherries dumping magnesium... that's what sugar does.

The treatment is ace inhibitors and low protein or soy protein because it reduces the proteinuria. The soy or flax are mimickers for estrogen.... that's where the value is, to keep the magnesium and put the calcium back where it belongs. Ideally, I am a fat eater.

I just have to try to fix this mess somehow. The soy also treats hyperlidema (my doctor wanted to put me on statins (127 weight) or have my weight at 118, leptin reset I lost the 5 or 6 lbs of belly fat and then filled out in the butt and thighs. I'm 5'8" and my classic weight is 119... I now weigh 130, but I think its fine. I need a new doctor, but this town is a sewing circle for the docs and their affiliations) ... which I think probably falls back to magnesium. But the enzymes aren't dumped.

I am now nervous about dha and soy. The test I read with the soy and fish oil being combined, the fish oil added no benefit. Maybe they compete for the same receptors, as does the flax and dha, (maybe estrogen and dha? shrug). I figure stop dumping calcium, add magnesium, and the fat solubles and when the pee is right... go back to the fish and fat. And keep using the sesamin.

The sticks can be bought off the internet to test with and I'll just go to the doctor when I'm "hot." And I guess it really is time to get very serious about hormone replacement. I was planning on waiting until the fall equinox and learning the moon chart... In the mean time, I'm going to use the bulgarian erection study sups, weak arginine and pycnogenol... both are blood pressure regulators and the pycnogenol is an ace inhibitor. I have low blood pressure compared to other people so I don't know how to get diagnosed other than urine. (although hyper lipids is a screaming sign... why statins?) There are some good meds, but hopefully, I won't need them.

I frequently have very flawed logic and constantly have infinite amounts of ignorance. If anyone has any experience with this, I sure would appreciate input.

For breakfast, I had bacon fried tofu and a magnesium sup.... I'm not sure if that was proper mixing of fats.

### chocolate — 2012-07-31T18:42:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-42063>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22208753](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22208753)

> Environ Int. 2012 Feb;39(1):134-40. Epub 2011 Nov 24.
>
> Estrogenic activity in extracts and exudates of cyanobacteria and green algae.
>
> SychrovÃ¡ E, ŠtěpÃ¡nkovÃ¡ T, NovÃ¡kovÃ¡ K, BlÃ¡ha L, Giesy JP, HilscherovÃ¡ K.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Research Centre for Toxic Compounds in the Environment (RECETOX), Faculty of Science, Masaryk University, Kamenice 126/3, 625 00 Brno, Czech Republic.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Here is presented some of the first information on interactions of compounds produced by cyanobacteria and green algae with estrogen receptor signaling. Estrogenic potency of aqueous extracts and exudates (culture spent media with extracellular products) of seven species of cyanobacteria (10 different laboratory strains) and two algal species were assessed by use of in vitro trans-activation assays. Compounds produced by cyanobacteria and algae, and in particular those excreted from the cells, were estrogenic. Most exudates were estrogenic with potencies expressed at 50% of the maximum response under control of the estrogen receptor ranging from 0.2 to 7.2 ng 17β-estradiol (E(2)) equivalents (EEQ)/L. **The greatest estrogenic potency was observed for exudates of Microcystis aerigunosa, a common species that forms water blooms. Aqueous extracts of both green algae, but only one species of cyanobacteria (Aphanizomenon gracile) elicited significant estrogenicity with EEQ ranging from 15 to 280 ng 17β-estradiol (E(2))/g dry weight.** Scenedesmus quadricauda exudates and extracts of Aphanizomenon flos-aquae were antagonistic to the ER when coexposed to E(2). The EEQ potency was not correlated with concentrations of cyanotoxins, such as microcystin and cylindrospermopsin, which suggests that the EEQ was comprised of other compounds. The study demonstrates some differences between the estrogenic potency of aqueous extracts prepared from the same species, but of different origin, while the effects of exudates were comparable within species. The observed estrogenic potencies are important namely in relation to the possible mass expansion of cyanobacteria and release of the active compounds into surrounding water.

I guess there might be sources of estrogen beside flax and soy.

### chocolate — 2012-07-31T21:30:48+0300

> A study shows that chlorella actively biotransforms estradiol into estrone and helps clear estrone as well – leaving alone estriol, your friendly antioxidant form of estrogen. This study suggests that any woman with a combination of digestive distress and high estrogen symptoms would benefit from taking chlorella – a simple solution that can be judged to be working by improvement in digestive and female symptoms.
>
>
> [http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/chlorella\_as\_a\_weight\_loss\_supplement/](http://http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/chlorella_as_a_weight_loss_supplement/)
>
> Several new animal studies show that chlorella is yet another nutritional tool to assist in the battle of the bulge. One study showed it could improve leptin and insulin function despite a high fat diet. Another study showed it prevents the accumulation of fat in fat cells (blunting the storage of calories).
>
>
>
> In the first study 9 weeks of chlorella supplementation1 lowered leptin levels in rats on a normal diet and in those on a high fat diet. Chlorella prevented the insulin resistance that otherwise occurs from a high fat diet. Furthermore, chlorella improved the functionality of glutamate transporters, which clear excess glutamate from nerves and thereby prevent excitotoxic damage to nerves. This is a novel finding since obesity is clearly linked to cognitive decline.

I guess I don't need soy, yet.

### chocolate — 2012-07-31T21:55:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-42099>

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/magnesium/](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/magnesium/)

> Nutrient Interactions
>
>
>
> Zinc
>
>
>
> **High doses of zinc in supplemental form apparently interfere with the absorption of magnesium**. One study reported that zinc supplements of 142 mg/day in healthy adult males significantly decreased magnesium absorption and disrupted magnesium balance (the difference between magnesium intake and magnesium loss) (4).
>
>
>
> Fiber
>
>
>
> Large increases in the intake of dietary fiber have been found to decrease magnesium utilization in experimental studies. However, the extent to which dietary fiber affects magnesium nutritional status in individuals with a varied diet outside the laboratory is not clear (2, 3).
>
>
>
> Protein
>
>
>
> Dietary protein may affect magnesium absorption. One study in adolescent boys found that magnesium absorption was lower when protein intake was less than 30 grams/day, and higher protein intakes (93 grams/day vs. 43 grams/day) were associated with improved magnesium absorption in adolescents (5).

> Isolation of an antihypertensive peptide from alcalase digest of Spirulina platensis.
>
> Lu J, Ren DF, Xue YL, Sawano Y, Miyakawa T, Tanokura M.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Applied Biological Chemistry, Graduate School of Agricultural and Life Sciences, The University of Tokyo, Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo, Japan.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> An angiotensin I-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitory peptide Ile-Gln-Pro with an IC(50) value of 5.77 +/- 0.09 microM was purified from the alcalase digests of Spirulina platensis by gel filtration chromatography and two steps of reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC). The peptide was synthesized and showed resistance to in vitro digestion by gastrointestinal proteases. Kinetics studies indicated that the peptide was a noncompetitive inhibitor and that the K(i) value was 7.61 +/- 0.16 microM. Oral administration of Ile-Gln-Pro at a dosage of 10 mg/kg showed significant decreases of the weighted systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) in spontaneously hypertensive rats (SHR) at 4, 6, and 8 h after treatment. **The results showed that the ACE inhibitory peptide from Spirulina platensis may have potential for use in the prevention and treatment of hypertension.**

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20509691](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20509691)

### chocolate — 2012-08-03T19:54:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-42720>

[http://www.benbest.com/health/kidney.html](http://http://www.benbest.com/health/kidney.html)

> Sodium reduction is often recommmended to prevent high blood pressure (especially in the elderly), but potassium/sodium ratio may be more important than absolute amount of sodium -- such that keeping potassium high may be at least as good a strategy as restricting sodium \[AMERICAN JOURNAL OF HYPERTENSION 12(8 Pt 1):849-851 (1999)\]. Potassium is also essential for nerve & muscle (including heart) function and for electrolyte balance. A tomato or banana provides about 600mg of potassium and a cup of milk or several large mushrooms provides about 400mg of the estimated 2000mg minimum adult daily potassium requirement. Kidney patients can benefit from potassium (and reduced blood pressure) as much as, if not more, than most people. But potassium is often restricted in kidney patients because a malfunctioning kidney may be unable to excrete potassium. If blood levels of potassium increase without limit, the patient is in danger of fatal cardiac arrest.
>
>
>
> Phosphorous is also restricted in kidney patients. For normal people phosphorous is as important as calcium for maintaining healthy bones, but for people with kidney disease high phosphorous can increase the harmful deposition of calcium in the kidney. Kidney patients have an increased requirement for calcium, but restrict dairy products because these foods are high in both calcium & phosphorous (meat is also high in phosphorous). Broccoli is high in both calcium & potassium, but does not have so much phosphorous. \[References: HUMAN NUTRITION H.Guthrie & M.Picciano MOSBY (1995) and MODERN NUTRITION IN HEALTH AND DISEASE M.Shils,et.al. LEA&FEBIGER (1994)\]

This guy has a pretty good collection of studies and concludes the only people that should watch their protein is kidney people. There is one study that shows the same amount of protein but fasting in between works. We're not in much of a position to play around with it yet, so we're going to count potassium and supplement accordingly. If you pee your brains out, obviously you need it. ### chocolate — 2012-08-04T23:57:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-42934>

We were down to two last eggs in the fridge and decided to fry them over easy and eat a yolk each with scads of bacon. I kind think bacon might be a carb sometimes. We didn't have anything else for breakfast and of course were starving by noon. We found a Thai peanut sauce and just substituted sunflower butter. Peanut butter has a much more domineering taste than the sunflower so we added lots of oil. It worked. We just used it for a dressing. Talk about filling you up quickly! We had a quarter head of ice berg chopped, the dregs out of a frozen shrimp bag, about two each and a very fatty ( that's just the we like 'em) leftover pork chop each chopped in. Probably about a tablespoon of sunflower butter and the same of oil and we got stuck. It had to be put away for later. That oily dressing is the ticket. I think guacamole works the same way. Crushing bacon atop would make it more interesting, and I'm really loving the idea of precooked bacon. I'm don't think this is going to be that challenging. No one is peeing neon yellow anymore, but just flying by the seat of our pants, the meat when overwhelmed with oil, seems fine. I'm surprise how suddenly it shuts down your appetite. And its not bad at all. I think no meat alternate days will be ok.

Peanut butter has a much more domineering taste than the sunflower so we added lots of oil. It worked. We just used it for a dressing. Talk about filling you up quickly! ### chocolate — 2012-08-05T21:03:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-43095>

We are trying a new n=1 about the lithium.

> Fewer metabolites of dietary choline reach the blood of rats after treatment with lithium.
>
> Pomfret EA, O'Connor SC, Zola TH, Zeisel SH.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Pathology, Boston University School of Medicine, MA 02118.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Choline is an important precursor for the biosynthesis of acetylcholine, phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin. It is also a major source of labile methyl groups. Lithium is an important component of the treatment of bipolar affective illness, and it inhibits choline transport across membranes. We studied the effect of lithium treatment upon the appearance in blood, liver and intestine of metabolites formed from dietary choline. Rats were treated for 9 days with 2 mEq/kg lithium carbonate or water. Animals were fasted overnight, and on the 10th day were fed with a solution containing radiolabeled choline chloride. The lithium-treated groups also received 2.0 mEq/kg lithium as part of this solution. After an oral dose of 1 ml of a 1 mM choline solution, the lithium-treated animals had significantly lower levels of choline-derived radiolabel in blood than did controls at 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes (47% (+/- 5%; SEM), 51% (+/- 7%), 59% (+/- 4%) and 74% (+/- 9%), respectively). We observed similar decreases of the accumulation in blood, at 180 minutes after the dose, of choline-derived radiolabel when choline was administered at lower or higher concentrations. After an oral treatment containing 0.1, 1 or 10 mM choline, lithium treated animals accumulated 69% (+/- 6%; SEM), 66% (+/- 11%) and 72% (+/- 7%) as much radiolabel in serum as did controls. Most of the radiolabel found in blood at 180 minutes was in metabolites of choline which are formed within liver (betaine and phosphatidylcholine). The diminished accumulation of radiolabel in serum after lithium treatment was not due to increased accumulation of label by erythrocytes, liver or gut wall. **We suggest that lithium influences the release by liver of betaine and phosphatidylcholine**.

We've decide to turn this on its ear and give the choline/inositol in the am and add the lithium oratate as needed. The stuff must be making the lithocholic acid somehow or something. You aren't supposed to be doing that unless you vitamin d is running full tilt. which obviously its not, because of the magnesium dump. The lithium orotate works so quickly that its not even an issue. The trouble with get off lithium is a dopamine rebound or so they say. No one knows why the stuff works as its killing you, I think its the mismatch of seasonal immune. I feel like a kid that can't wait for Christmas waiting on those pee cups. I hate certain large populations of doctors.

We did an n=1 on the coffee and paid close attention and its definitely something that screws with your electrolytes. Maybe we'll get an extract and add it to something for the polyphenols. I'm thinking there is nothing better than a hopped up vitamin d system on the natch. Think how cheap and easy that would be. In the meantime, we don't know which way to lean the hardest at night. > Decreased PAA levels were found in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. This level was reversed significantly after treatment with vitamin C along with metformin compared to placebo with metformin. FBS, PMBG, and HbA1c levels showed significant improvement after 12 weeks of treatment with vitamin C. In conclusion, oral supplementation of vitamin C with metformin reverses ascorbic acid levels, reduces FBS, PMBG, and improves HbA1c. Hence, both the drugs in combination may be used in the treatment of type 2 DM to maintain good glycemic control.

> This is cool about vitamin c:

There is a terrible muggy heat event in LA, and since we're on the Valley side of the Hollywood Hills, we get it 10 degrees hotter than the beach side. I don't have so many chores. ### chocolate — 2012-08-08T19:55:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-43695>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11830761](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11830761)

> CNS Drug Rev. 2001 Winter;7(4):471-81.
>
> Phenibut (beta-phenyl-GABA): a tranquilizer and nootropic drug.
>
> Lapin I.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Clinical and Experimental Psychopharmacology, Bekhterev's Psychoneurological Institute, Bekhterev Street, 3, St. Petersburg, 193019, Russia. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Phenibut (**beta-phenyl-gamma-aminobutyric acid HCl) is a neuropsychotropic drug that was discovered and introduced into clinical practice in Russia in the 1960s. It has anxiolytic and nootropic (cognition enhancing) effects. It acts as a **GABA-mimetic, primarily at GABA(B) and, to some extent, at GABA(A) receptors. It also stimulates dopamine receptors and antagonizes beta-phenethylamine** (PEA), a putative endogenous anxiogenic. The psychopharmacological activity of phenibut is similar to that of baclofen, a p-Cl-derivative of phenibut. This article reviews the structure-activity relationship of phenibut and its derivatives. Emphasis is placed on the importance of the position of the phenyl ring, the role of the carboxyl group, and the activity of optical isomers. Comparison of phenibut with piracetam and diazepam reveals similarities and differences in their pharmacological and clinical effects. Phenibut is widely used in Russia to relieve tension, anxiety, and fear, to improve sleep in psychosomatic or neurotic patients; as well as a pre- or post-operative medication. It is also used in the therapy of disorders characterized by asthenia and depression, as well as in post-traumatic stress**, stuttering and vestibular disorders.

I got some of this stuff from Happy Pills Almagamated without researching. I thought I'd better look it up. Glad I did. Its a GABA mimicker and is HCL. That Betaine is looking better and better. So many things to figure out. I guess it is a rabbit hole. You can't make GABA without enough stomach acid it seems. And you don't get enough of that, unless you have enough lithium. Well, at least the magnesium stops headaches.

### chocolate — 2012-08-10T21:08:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-im-thinking-about-going-over-to-the-dark-side.2766/post-44176>

Well, my peanut brain has been noodling about anti-oxidants and the blood brain barrier.

[http://www.pslgroup.com/dg/4a3a2.htm](http://http://www.pslgroup.com/dg/4a3a2.htm)

{QUOTE\]

Vitamin C Shown To Cross The Blood Brain Barrier

NEW YORK, NY -- December 1, 1997 -- The blood brain barrier has long been regarded as the body's most formidable gatekeeper. It is a virtual fortress of blood vessels that forms a protective barrier between the blood and brain, screening any chemical that attempts to access the brain's inner sanctum. But the blood brain barrier's protective role can be a drawback, as it also blocks access to substances that would be good for the brain. One such substance is vitamin C, an antioxidant that is essential to keep the central nervous system functioning properly.

Now, researchers at Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center have discovered how to get large amounts of vitamin C past the blood brain barrier so that it is transported and retained in the brains of laboratory mice. This finding may prove useful in efforts to slow the progression of certain neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's. The investigators report their findings in today’s issue of the Journal of Clinical Investigation.

"We now know how to get large amounts of an antioxidant into the brain," said Dr. David Agus, an oncologist at Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center and lead author of the study.

The researchers studied vitamin C absorption in the brain to determine why it was present in this organ's tissue at higher concentrations than any other area of the body. Earlier research by Dr. David Golde, physician-in-chief of Memorial Hospital, and his colleagues had established that specific glucose transporter molecules were responsible for transporting vitamin C into cells. This process occurs when vitamin C, which is used by cells in the form of ascorbic acid, is converted into the form of dehydroascorbic acid and transported into the cell. Once inside, the vitamin is converted back to ascorbic acid.

Building on this research, Drs. Agus, Golde and their colleagues reasoned that vitamin C would cross the blood brain barrier as dehydroascorbic acid via the same glucose transport mechanism and be retained as ascorbic acid in the brain. To find out, mice were injected with either ascorbic acid, dehydroascorbic acid, or sucrose (as a measure of blood volume) and their brains were subsequently analyzed at varying time intervals for vitamin C content. The researchers found that ascorbic acid was not able to cross the blood brain barrier, while dehydroascorbic acid readily entered the brain and was retained in the tissue as ascorbic acid.

Although scientists do not know the exact role that vitamin C plays in the brain, recent studies have shown that various vitamin compounds with antioxidant-like properties can slow the progression of moderately severe Alzheimer's disease. In addition, vitamin C is also known to act as a scavenger of free radicals -- substances that play a role in causing diseases. \[/QUOTE\] I stopped short of the good info because my computer is acting up. But its been studied.

I was just looking up cholecalciferol... Vitamin D. Anticholinergenics suppress this. Interesting.[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cholecalciferol](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cholecalciferol)

> "Vitamin D toxicity can result from regular excess intake of this vitamin, and may lead to hypercalcemia and excess bone loss. Individuals at particular risk include those with hyperparathyroidism, kidney disease, sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, or histoplasmosis. Chronic hypercalcemia may lead to serious or even life-threatening complications, and should be managed by a physician. **Early symptoms of hypercalcemia may include nausea, vomiting, and anorexia (appetite/weight loss), followed by polyuria (excess urination), polydipsia (excess thirst), weakness, fatigue, somnolence, headache, dry mouth, metallic taste, vertigo, tinnitus (ear ringing), and ataxia (unsteadiness).** Kidney function may become impaired, and metastatic calcifications (calcium deposition in organs throughout the body) may occur, particularly affecting the kidneys. Treatment involves stopping the intake of vitamin D or calcium, and lowering the calcium levels under strict medical supervision, with frequent monitoring of calcium levels. Acidification of urine and corticosteroids may be necessary."\[12\]
>
>
>
> There are conflicting reports concerning the absorption of cholecalciferol (D3) versus ergocalciferol (D2), with some studies suggesting less efficacy of D2,\[13\] and others showing no difference.\[14\] At present, D2 and D3 doses are frequently considered interchangeable, but more research is needed to clarify this.
