### [email redacted] — 2012-08-05T17:13:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43054>

I've been re-reading the blogs that address cortisol and am having a hard time figuring it out. So far this is what I understand:

You need T3 to convert hormones. I have low T3 & T4 so have been taking Armour for 3 months now. Right now I am at 1/2 grain.

But Jack says you will drive up your RT3 if you have high cortisol and are taking Armour. But when he says high cortisol, does he mean at any one time like at night or does he mean if it is high throughout the day? I felt like my first set of labs were OK except in the evening and at night. Now my night is looking better but all the rest went up. I feel like I'm spinning my wheels.

Gonna keep reading......

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-05T17:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43060>

You can produce rT3 with cortisol that is higher than it should be (like yours). Have you tested your rT3?

1/2 grain of Armour is a super-duper low dose. Have you tested your FT3 to see if it is getting higher?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-05T17:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43065>

I'm getting my FT3, FT4, RT3 retested next week. I'm anxious to see the results. I asked for an increase last month, but the doc wanted to give it another month and retest before upping it. But again, with these cortisol levels wouldn't increasing my dose be useless? I started taking PS about 2 weeks prior to testing, so I don't think it had a chance to make a difference. I just don't know how else to fix cortisol. I'm not doing artificial light at night and I am making sure I get sun every morning. I haven't been stressed at all lately. The kids are actually behaving and doing pretty well. What else?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-05T18:18:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43069>

How's your hs CRP? Inflammation anywhere could be driving your cortisol up I think.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43073>

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-06T02:51:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/classic-pregnenolone-steal.2847/post-43149>

Started 10mg DHEA & 200mg pregnenolone 6 weeks prior to the latest cortisol test & 5 weeks prior to root canal. Upped the DHEA to 20mg 3 weeks prior to cortisol test. The root canal was about 2 weeks prior to cortisol test.

The DHEA & pregnenolone results that I posted are prior to supplementing anything and was drawn on day 3 of cycle. I am taking micronized DHEA & micronized pregnenolone from Nutricology. I am taking both with breakfast in the morning.

I am on 1g of estrogen, split between morning & noon. I am taking 200mg progesterone day 12-26 of cycle. My cycles are lasting about 42-44 days so I'm thinking the progesterone is not working. Would have thought the pregnenolone would help but apparently it is being shunted to cortisol, so not really helping.

I feel like I'm doing everything else right. I'm eating right (NEVER cheat), I'm mindful of circadian cycle (lights out at 7), eating within 30 minutes of waking, not exercising yet (though I am going to start walking more).

I have an appt with a doctor in Atlanta next week to see if he can help me with my gut. After which, I will be setting up a consult with Jack to discuss my options and see if I can determine the best course of action.

# Thread: Healing achilles tendon pain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-08-04T04:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-42804>

Earlier this year, my right achilles tendon began hurting--no injury or strain or anything, no prior problems with it before, and it's only my right foot. It comes and goes; I can't really tell what might set it off, but going up and down the stairs is extremely painful when it hurts. I thought CT'ing might help, but it hasn't, and neither have Epsom salt soaks. Is there something else I can be doing? I really don't want to see a doctor for it yet.

### Huck — 2012-08-04T05:04:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-42806>

I had that happen about a year ago and ordered an insert for my shoes. I guess it worked because when I saw your post, I realized I haven't had any pain in months. The brand I bought was SuperFeet. There are also exercises you can do.

### MightyAl — 2012-08-04T22:32:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-42899>

Achilles tendonitis is no joke. I fought it for a year but it was due to a heavy running regimen. The injury usually occurs from a repetitive exercise.

I fixed my issues by not running for six months and switching to minimilist shoes when I did run and going barefoot whenever I had the opportunity. Anything with a raised heel will exacerbate the problem.

### Susan M. — 2012-08-05T03:55:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-42997>

I fought it for over 20 years. I also discovered, a little over two years ago, that I have a moderate bone spur. Chronic pain is no fun. I finally had to go to a doctor who ordered me orthotics for my shoes. I was also told to stay off my feet as much as I could until the inflammation came down. I used icing and exercises. I also now only use really good shoes. I was a shoe minimalist growing up, but I have to have a slight lift in my shoes. I simply cannot go barefoot or use flats for any length of time. My doctor said most of the problem came from the way that I walked. Anyway, I followed his advise and have been pain free for about 1 1/2 years. I am walking up to four miles a day without pain. If I even think I am starting to have pain in my feet, I get off of them as soon as I am able and give them a rest.

### artsygirl — 2012-08-06T13:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-43176>

Same experienced I had but this tip helped a lot me getting better. Treatment is a course of anti-inflammatory drugs, use of a heel cup, and alternating ice and heat therapy. Stretching the Achilles tendon is very important and should be emphasized. In severe cases, patients can wear an open-back shoe to reduce pain

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-06T14:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-43180>

magnesium oil over the tendon twice a day is something one might consider.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-10T02:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-44011>

I don't know if this is helpful. I have had one surgery to repair my Achilles (7 years ago, only half successful, I would say!) and still waiting for the "right" time to do the other (had 2 babies in the meantime!). One thing that is oh-so-clear right now: my Achilles problems are directly tied to inflammation in my body. When I eat incorrectly (or increase inflammation in other ways), it directly and immediately impacts my Achilles. I don't how or why this happens, but they are tied together. So, I would say that anything that reduces inflammation for you will have some kind of positive impact upon your Achilles. So, IMO, do everything you can to reduce inflammation and related neolithic disease and you will helping your Achilles issue at the same time.

### janagram — 2012-08-11T04:48:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-44259>

take this or toss it, but I healed mine by wearing Crocs! And I told a friend ....who then had the same result!

### Glamazon — 2012-08-12T00:01:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-44407>

I have had problems with my achilles for about 3 years. One day while walking I felt a "give" in my right achilles and I have had pain ever since. It is the only pain that has not gone away since going epi paleo in February of this year. My massage therapist was feeling it in late May and called it "mushy" to touch. In July I added DHEA to my regimen, large doses, and I just realized yesterday that it is gone.

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T12:48:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-54385>

You might benefit from having some fluoros done, even standard xrays as well. I have bilateral calcaneal spurs however, my right foot has three broken spurs, one of which is located at the insertional point of the tendon itself.

Thanks to free consults where I work, the PT gave me a list of exercises to do while Im at the gym, and I definitely needed a heel-raise insert for my shoes, Im doing about 90% pain free.

Talk with your PCP first (if you have an HMO) to see if you can get an order for radiology. He/She might even recommend you seek Podiatric care. Most Podiatrists are credentialed to do their own in-house fluoros... for extreme cases, they will also send you for rads as well.

### René Borg — 2014-11-20T16:43:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-147976>

I’m a movement coach and I treat Achilles injuries on a weekly basis so here’s my take on it:

Part of what makes all chronic injuries – such as the various forms of Achilles pain – difficult to treat is that they are multi-factorial. The original cause tends to be straightforward enough – some tissue abuse (see below) – but what maintains and perpetuates the symptoms – and stops them from healing can be related to all of the factors that are being discussed on these forums.

Traditional sports medicine will generally refer to most injuries such as these as ‘overuse’ but it’s my experience that ‘misuse’ or ‘disuse’ are much more apt descriptions of the problem. To distinguish:

· Overuse: Either an ‘acute overload’ (one huge run, one lift way beyond capability) or repetitive overload (which is generally – under-recovery – i.e. the load is always just a bit larger than what the recovery you take can build back, so your tissues get weaker and weaker and organism more and more run down etc.)

· Misuse: Inappropriate loading of the tissues – to use a metaphor – it’s like asking the engineer to do the job of the architect on a building project or asking one workman to take on the job of two workmen. Eventually you get some bad results from this. Most injuries are caused and maintained because of this in my personal experience. It also fits with the mismatch/evolutionary medicine angle on sports injuries (I’ll elaborate below)

· Disuse: This is part of the chronic injury cycle – the pain you feel in your Achilles will cause you to either cease all movement that causes pain or it will cause your body to try and avoid movement in areas of the body it considers under the threat. The end result is the same: the tissue go weaker not stronger. This is why the great movement teachers of our age – such as Ido Portal – will say ‘rest, I do not believe in rest’ – meaning: you have to recover back into movement as inactivity can never create a stimulus to make your tissues stronger. There are catastrophic injuries (acute Achilles tear) that does not allow immediate return to movement but these are rare and general Achilles pain does not fall into that category. That said: you need to be very smart, or work with a very smart coach, to introduce the right movement back at the right rate.

So in terms of the biomechanical breakdowns that lead to ‘misuse injury’ of the Achilles tendon. Dr Dudley Morton really had all of this figured out in his original book ‘The Human Foot’. His basic finding was that the big toe joint and the first metatarsal joint are the keys to stable and safe movement on two feet. Probably 99 out of 100 runners I see have the foot problems, today, that Dr Morton identified as being a primary cause of almost every single activity-related injury out there that involves the lower legs – including ankle, knee, hip and lower back pain. Dr Janet Travell expounded on this further when she found a connection between the malfunction of the foot and pretty much all trigger points, myofascial pain, ‘hotspots’ (whatever you want to call them).

Here’s what happens: during both running and walking there is a moment where the foot must roll inwards (normal pronation) allowing the plantar fascia arch to collapse. The first metatarsal is designed to arrest this motion and absorb the majority of the force involved in the movement (your bodyweight x momentum). When the first metatarsal is displaced (as in pushed inwards or behind the second metatarsal) or is hypermobile (not stable enough) this causes overpronation – or an excessive inward roll. There are other factors in this (such as bad posture) – but let’s keep it simple for now and focus on the foundation of your house – the feet. Tight hips etc. need to be fixed – but one thing at a time.

Upwards in the chain, the leg and hip muscles as well as postural stabilisers need to try and control this excessive force. The posterior tibialis muscle is one that often gets overworked here trying to stop this excessive roll and you’ll find trigger points in it if this is the case. But it doesn’t have to stop there – you may find these points everywhere all the way up into your glutes and upper back. Where do most trigger points in the lower leg refer pain? You guessed it – the Achilles tendon. A quick fix is to get dry needling done – by far the quickest and cheapest way to get rid of trigger points although self-treatment is an option (but it requires a lot of persistence and is still only treating the symptoms – not the root cause). Removing trigger point is often necessary to be able to reintroduce the right movement without excess tension (i.e. if I teach someone how to land properly but their calf is all knotted up I cannot get the right level of muscle relaxation for barefoot landing. So I need to remove the trigger points, then coach the movement and then have the client practice it).

The long-term fix starts with ‘fixing your feet’ – one way to do this is regular ‘toe drills’ as invented by Lee Saxby – we have a link to our page on it here:

This needs to be combined with coaching on proper landing, jumping etc. with a movement coach and a gradual change into footwear that is flat (high-heeled shoes will continue to create a shortened position of the Achilles and excess tension in the calf), hard (soft material under the sole tenses up the plantar arch again increasing tension over the ankle joint) and wide (the big toe needs space to move back into its natural fully splayed position). Remember: most modern shoes provide aan evolutionary mismatch – all children under 4 that I see have perfect feet. Over 4 it’s a different story – the foot was designed to be wide and flat – and shoe designs would ideally support that which few brands do today (Vivobarefoot a notable exception).

Finally, even when the Achilles tendon has begun to rot (degeneration of the tissue – loss of quality in the collagen fibres what is called tendinosis) or there is some micro-tearing in the collagen fibres or chronic inflammation around the sheath or in the bursas (such as retrocalceneal bursitis – often mistaken for Achilles pain), focusing on fixing your feet first and then being coached on correct landing, jumping, walking and running and practicing those micro-skills will create the right signal for your Achilles tendon to begin to heel. Slight friction massage also works because it helps desensitise the brain to the inflammation and pain the area. You can look up many techniques yourself (just stroke the tendon gently across the fibres and then work up the intensity or plug a tennis ball into the tendon, press down and rotate it). You’ll need to be doing this for at least 10 days for at least 10-20 minutes of focused effort to expect results – otherwise go to a manual therapist for a series (don’t expect results from such work in one session). It has to be focused and not willy-nilly – otherwise it’s like trying to shout down a thunderstorm. Your positive signal has to be stronger than the negative signals that created the injuries in the first place.

This entire process I described begins a vicious circle that is too complex to describe here – but to give you one example – because your foot is no longer a stable surface your subconscious brain will generate excessive tension in many systems (and in some cases pain) to try and stop, control or force you to cease the damaging movement. So the malfunctioning foot is the trigger. New movement patterns begin to form – that are sub-optimal – and over time these become your ‘default software’ rather than original ‘natural software’ you were built with and that you developed as a child through motor skill milestones. These new motor patterns then create new injuries and pains and the circle continues.

When these injuries don’t heal – even after foot mechanics and movement patterns have been fixed with the help of a coach, then it’s worthwhile asking all the questions of yourself that this blog opens up such as “am I living and eating in a way that allows my body to heal and regenerate” or “have I triggered some kind of chronical pain syndrome through hormonal breakdown” etc. etc.

Rene, have you ever considered the quantum biology of tendon function? How sunlight, water, and magnetism affect the achilles heel?

### AJT — 2014-11-27T17:34:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-148643>

laser works, ART is extremely helpful, if you use a heel lift you must understand that you are altering the functionality of the tendon and shortening both the gastrocs, and soleus, and they will degrade from a muscular and neurological standpoint. Rather than change your entire skeletal dynamic you can make very deliberate changes to footware, walking, and efforts to restore proper tendon and muscle length.

### René Borg — 2015-02-16T20:11:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-155806>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=147980)
>
> Rene, have you ever considered the quantum biology of tendon function? How sunlight, water, and magnetism affect the achilles heel?

hi nonchalant, I think it would be more accurate to say I am coming to terms with the full implications using those laws as a filter has on the treatment of sports injuries.

At the moment the theory of biotensegrity is laying waste to a lot of cherished notions about how biomechanics works (and even where the borders of the field itself should be) and one thing that really stands out to me when you read Jack's stuff and then put on the biotensegrity glasses, so essentially that 'the skeleton' floats in an ocean of muscle, tendon, fascia etc. most of which is made of collagen and most of which again is made of water, is that any of the 'suboptimal states' Jack describes on these pages could be a primary culprit for the injury - whether it be worsened reduction potential or chronic inflammatory states. So while movement happens to be my speciality I am under no illusion that every pain can be fixed by correcting the 'movement problem' even if it is relevant to the original injury - if the other factors necessary for healing or for creating an adaptable organism are not there, the pain may persist and, in the example of this thread, the Achilles tissue will not heal properly.

We recently took the full consequence of this and hooked up with Barry Murray who also posts here in a partnership called Primal3. The basic motivation was that we needed some way of helping people address movement, lifestyle and nutrition side-by-side rather than going to specialists in isolation - so we're trying to give people a template that get's them started with the basics in all three arenas. It's really borne out of a frustration that you can sometimes create a perfect movement machine (in terms of a person's understanding of close to perfect motor control and skill execution) but you can still either find pain or even see visible tension. A particular runner springs to mind who has incredibly graceful movement but he is both weakly, pale and ill looking and disposed to regular niggles. He is also a vegan and heavily exposed to EMF and while I don't want to use the example to bash the diet, my feeling is that adopting the epi-Paleo diet and taking more precautions to avoid modern stressors would bring this particular athlete back on track. From a movement perspective there is little more I can do for him - because even strengthening the good movement pattern through conditioning would likely fail as his organism would be able to absorb the stress.

### nonchalant — 2015-02-16T20:25:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-155807>

I have been exploring the tensegrity aspect of health since Jack brought it up. It's really exciting to discover how my body reacts to various stimuli. And how a burst of energy from the collagen can solve some physical problems very quickly. I guess the secret, like he says, is to preload the system, so that it can respond to the stimulus with the production of energy.

Your athlete is probably too dehydrated and doesn't have enough DHA to produce the needed energy from stimulus. He must be running through his stem cells like crazy, sorry to say.

### René Borg — 2015-02-17T18:24:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-155910>

Yes, completely agree. I coach many IT professionals (who often are very driven to run long distances, hard races or both) - and I have no doubt that people would get away with a lot of the poor movement they are doing if their cells had better tensegrity cytoarchitecture in the cell as Jack talks about in Tensegrity 1 and easy to see why IT workers are getting murdered here by EMF effects (just to start the list of stressors the average Blue chip worker has to absorb before he even hits the road). I'd compare an office worker to a really stiff and flat ball whereas the Kenyan is like hard-pumped bouncy ball.

That doesn't mean we should be happy with the poor movement but essentially in Kenyans you can get away with just 'polishing the chrome' (technique wise) and still keep injury free despite enormous training volume at very high quality. The gap between what these athletes can do and the average weekend warrior is so great that biomechanics alone is definitely not the answer - it has to sit within trying to replicate and evolutionary environment around the 'would-be' athlete.

Nonchalant have you looked into Stephen Levin's work on Biotensegrity and Dynamic Anatomy? [http://www.biotensegrity.com/tensegrity\_new\_biomechanics.php](http://www.biotensegrity.com/tensegrity_new_biomechanics.php)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-17T21:10:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-155928>

If you want to see Tensegrity at its base function watch here in the video when Pollack gets to the Nafion tube filled with water building the EZ and how it deflects the electric field.......that deflection is collagen in a cell. 13:49 of this video

### René Borg — 2015-08-26T13:11:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-achilles-tendon-pain.2849/post-175774>

Thanks for the video Jack, trying to wrap this into terms I can understand: so collagen is a family of 28 proteins that acts as a glue within a lot of our bodily structures and is a key player in healing damage. So when you say the deflection is collagen in a cell, does that mean that collagen is essentially forming the tube that organises the water. I have heard mention that this enables 'proton-jump induction' the effect of which is basically poorer inter-cellular communication channels.

So if the body is in a state where the collagen doesn't function properly, we get poorer internal coordination and communication?

From an observation perspective this seems to be one reason that an athlete with 'misaligned collagen bonds' (as a body worker might observe it) in a formerly shredded Achilles shows lack of proprioception in the ankle area - because the communication signals from that area of the body - molecules and cells communicating internally - are destroyed.

Do you see value in the traditional methods of realigning collagen fibres through movements that force them back into a certain position or alignment or body work (such as rolfing, trigger point etc.) which seems to do something similar? Or would you say that we can solve this almost entirely by 'fixing the terrain' (and letting the body relay it on its own).

Am I way off in my understanding?

# Thread: Cold Thermogenesis during the winter?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-08-04T16:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-42834>

Are we supposed to do cold thermogenesis during the winter? I was reading through the faq and I didn't see any mentioned of it.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-04T18:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-42841>

I'm new to this but yes we are supposed to do CT in winter. Supposedly it is even more beneficial in winter than summer, maybe due to the immunity benefits.

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-04T19:01:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-42860>

damn, that is going to be pretty difficult. I don't use the heater by default since the air helps also in becoming cold adaptive.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-05T03:28:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-42990>

Yeah I know what you mean, that is why I'm so glad I started this in summer when it's probably easier to start CT'ing.  by winter I'm hoping to be fully cold adapted.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-05T06:51:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43011>

I find it easier when it's colder. Summer makes it hard.... tap is too warm. In winter I just run the tap and there's my 50 degrees or less ct tub. IN summer I have to spend money... on ice. not fun.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-05T08:09:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43016>

My tap comes out at 53 right now, makes me wonder how much colder it will be in winter!! And it's really hot here right now, still comes out 53.

### kathylu — 2012-08-05T14:47:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43029>

> **NWgirl;43298 wrote:** My tap comes out at 53 right now, makes me wonder how much colder it will be in winter!! And it's really hot here right now, still comes out 53.

Lucky you! My tap water is coming out at 77 degrees now. Can't wait for winter!

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T14:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43032>

I started CT in the mid of February. I was doing OK until I took a break for a month due to a big family visit from overseas in May. I got back in June but it has been much harder than at the beginning. Maybe it is the winter/summer issue?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-06T02:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43148>

> **NWgirl;43298 wrote:** My tap comes out at 53 right now, makes me wonder how much colder it will be in winter!! And it's really hot here right now, still comes out 53.

Do you have a well? I have a well and if I run the hose outside first to remove the warmed water in the pipes I can get to 53. In the winter I can get to 49 so not much difference except I don't have to run the water much first. Hmmm, wonder how cold it would be if I did run it good first?

### AKMan — 2012-08-06T23:26:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43281>

I think in the grand scheme of things, a long-range-plan if you will, it is best to CT October through March, peaking your CT activities in late January, then tapering off until early April. This coincides with the normal temperature range and light cycles. Apr - Sep one should enjoy seasonally available fruits, warm weather, and outdoor activities to maximize Vit D.

If you are overcoming health problems it's OK to jump into CT whenever, but I think the long term goal should be seasonality.

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-08T02:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43536>

so do CT through October - March and stop there?

Also I dont use even my heating system, my place is cold and it is pure cold. I just wear sweaters. Kinda like our ancestors wearing animal fur from the prey they killed.

### AKMan — 2012-08-08T02:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43541>

> **x\_danny\_x;43827 wrote:** so do CT through October - March and stop there?
>
>
>
> Also I dont use even my heating system, my place is cold and it is pure cold. I just wear sweaters. Kinda like our ancestors wearing animal fur from the prey they killed.

Where abouts do you live?

Unless you are trying to overcome some serious hormonal/health problems, seasonal cold is fine, and maybe better. I live in Alaska and have access to very cold air Oct-Mar. The tendency is to never experience cold, even in a place like this...bundle up when outside, heat on full-blast in car, thermostat set on 72 in the house. Last winter, I kept thermostat in the 60's, wore just enough clothes to prevent frostbite and drove with heat off. Never felt better! This summer, I haven't intentionally CT'd, but took a few swims in 50 degree water and our air temp is rarely over 70--lots of 40's and 50's.

Endless Summer is the cycle we need to break!

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-08T15:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43634>

I live in New York. My apartment is like in the 50s or sometimes less for temperture. Turning on the heating system just racks up utility bills sky high. That is why I don't do it. New York is expensive to live.

Do I need to take CT baths if my apartment is that cold to begin with? I just wear a sweater, a hat, and maybe a hoody thin jacket. Even with that I still feel the cold.

I am scared if I take Cold baths while my apartment is already 50 degrees will give rise to hyperthermia or something.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-08T15:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43639>

keep the apt cold and jump in the cold water.......that is a win win.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-08T20:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43696>

> **x\_danny\_x;43925 wrote:**
>
> Do I need to take CT baths if my apartment is that cold to begin with? I just wear a sweater, a hat, and maybe a hoody thin jacket. Even with that I still feel the cold.
>
>
>
> I am scared if I take Cold baths while my apartment is already 50 degrees will give rise to hyperthermia or something.

I think you'll be surprised Danny. If you follow the CT protocol, and take it step by step, your body will respond. I know that you can't yet imagine sitting in ice water and not shivering, but it can be done. You just have to follow the rules. And you will experience a freedom from fear of the cold. You will know that your body will take care of it with ease. In fact, early on, your body will over react, and you will find yourself turning on a fan to cool off. It's amazing. It's a real attitude adjustment.

### AKMan — 2012-08-08T20:29:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-43703>

> **x\_danny\_x;43925 wrote:** I live in New York. My apartment is like in the 50s or sometimes less for temperture. Turning on the heating system just racks up utility bills sky high. That is why I don't do it. New York is expensive to live.
>
>
>
> Do I need to take CT baths if my apartment is that cold to begin with? I just wear a sweater, a hat, and maybe a hoody thin jacket. Even with that I still feel the cold.
>
>
>
> I am scared if I take Cold baths while my apartment is already 50 degrees will give rise to hyperthermia or something.

You do need to be mindful of hypothermia. All the CT'ing in the world won't make you immune to feeling the cold or dieing if you get too cold. CT'ing will train your body to better deal with the cold and increase circulation, etc... Please read up on hypothermia and know the warning signs. It sounds like you may live alone--be very careful you don't get in trouble here! You may want to get someone else involved if you take the cold bath plunge.

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-11T18:18:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-44340>

wow man. im just better get started now then. right now is imagining me doing this. in the cold and taking cold baths for 2 hours.

i do live alone. hypothermia is my concern. going to be nuts though.

one question, Jack just mentioned it is a win win. I was wondering though when I become cold adaptive, can I walk outside in the winter with just a sweater on or a t-shirt? What is the limit cold temp that I can do that?

I try to get my family involved, but they are too brainwashed with what they think is truly healthy. They think eating grains, avoiding cholesterol, eating Carbs during the winter, etc. I have no one in case something happens.

### caroline — 2012-08-12T14:37:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-44504>

I am by no means an expert on C/T - but I think you might be over thinking this. Start slowly. reread the easy start guide for C/T. You don't have to spend 2 hours in a cold bath. Start with 20 minutes. Actually did you know that you should start with face dunks? I look forward to hearing about your progress. I don't have any heat on - just wear sweaters. Lately I seem to only manage cold showers and ice packs but at least it is something. It is winter here in OZ.

Good luck!

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-13T16:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-44682>

I know the protocol. I do face dunks and spot icing. I went to the cold shower and can handle it for 30 minitues. I can handle a bath though for 45-1 hour minutes in 60 degree water though. Im trying to get to 2 hours by time winter starts in New York. It is currently summer here.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-14T17:31:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-44901>

Sounds like you're doing great so far, Danny!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-28T01:06:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-47570>

Just realized with my 'all inclusive' apt. I am moving to...the heat will be blasted sky high with roomies are sensitive to cold (and would have been me too in the past...and I was the worst offender for needing HEAT). Now....it will be too hot. LOL I will have to at least keep my bedroom window open. I will have to 'stay outside' more often then too. I mean in the evenings, someone suggested a fire (or some form of amber lighting appropriate for city dwelling) so as to remove further artificial lighting from technology during winter when it gets dark so early.

What I tend to do when it's 0-10'C...is I can wear shorts and t-shirt but I need a hat, gloves and maybe boots. I look funny but am more comfortable that way. It's been so long since 'winter' since we had an early 'summer' since March (in S. Ontario). (Same as NYC). So the thought of winter is so foreign to me at the moment. lol

Cooler Fall temps are quite refreshing though...although 20'C is not 'cool'...but cooler than 30+.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T01:31:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-during-the-winter.2853/post-47585>

go outside in the lake.......

# Thread: Holy hormones!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-08-04T18:32:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42843>

So suddenly this month my hormones have taken off. I've had more acne at the beginning of this cycle it's crazy. One zit will show up then after it goes away another will show up in its place! And they're all been the Big Cystic acne kind

I think 2 things are going on. 1) I changed the application site of my BHRT & 2) I think the epi-paleo is pushing some detox from me.

Any one else experiencing this????

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-04T18:39:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42851>

Dr K says there is no detox from epi-paleo.... not sure I can support that statement . I had a coated tongue for the last week which is now starting to clear. But hopefully it will affect your hormones... in a good way!

### shilohman — 2012-08-04T18:47:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42854>

The epi-paleo diet may not be causing any detox, but your up regulated hormone levels might be. You hormones may be causing you to lean out and in the process you are getting detox from this effect. The epi-paleo diet may be contributing to all of this with the increased nutrients etc. That's my two cents.

### colleencoble — 2012-08-04T19:31:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42869>

It's likely changing the site of the application. That makes a HUGE difference. Take a look at how much DHEA you're taking. That's the most common cause of the zits and you may be ready to lower it now.

### Melinda — 2012-08-04T19:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42871>

I'm new to BHRT and inner thigh was the suggested application site, progest in the pm and estro in the am. Does that sound right?

### kathylu — 2012-08-04T19:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42875>

Another question. Since I am trying to do BHRT on the cheap...I haven't signed up with Dzugan for instance...I took his published protocols to my doc. She agreed to do the scripts, but I'm on my own otherwise, as this is the first time she has prescribed BHRT. So, where are the best application sites? I have read lots of conflicting info on this. Some say inner arm, others inner thighs, some say rub progesterone on the breasts, some say never do that, some say no hormones ever above the umbilicus, etc. So, what do you do and why? Thanks.

### colleencoble — 2012-08-04T21:31:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42888>

No that's not good, Melinda. Anything below the waist doesn't absorb well. I've found the inner part of the upper arm is the best absorption site. That's where I had Grech change to. I take progesterone and estrogen AM and PM both but then my hormones were very broken and I had no ovaries.

Kathy, I've tried all absorption sites in the past 6 years and the hormones seem to work best as applied above. When I try rubbing progesterone on the breasts, it doesn't absorb well and I start getting estrogen dominant (breasts get sore)

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-04T21:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-42892>

> **colleencoble;43148 wrote:** It's likely changing the site of the application. That makes a HUGE difference. Take a look at how much DHEA you're taking. That's the most common cause of the zits and you may be ready to lower it now.

I'm taking 15mg of DHEA - my last set of tests, (in may and the ASI in July) my DHA was out of bounds on the high ends.... hehehe in the past it barely registered! LOL! same for pregnenolone as well... hoping to see positive move fwd in the Pg:E2 ratios...

### natural_girl — 2012-08-05T15:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-43034>

Glad things are moving along for you Gretchen! Mine have been a bit slower going if you recall I had a lot of issues with the PG right at the beginning and had to back down for awhile. (I think some was converting to cortisol & it was just to much to soon, I could fall asleep anytime I sat down)

I am just doing the PG right now I told him I did not want to do the Triest until I got my ratio better. Are you still doing any Triest?

I will be going for blood work soon going to get everything tested want to see what my thyroid is up to also and where my RT3 is and if I should increase or discontinue use of the T3. I am ordering an ASI test too this week and got my orange glasses using those along with seafood one meal per day. (That is all I can really afford right now)

I think I might have found a missing key! I have been taking HCL for about 2 years now but only 2 sometimes 3 per meal. Well, JK said I should up that in our consult and I have been slowly doing that. The last few days I have been taking 5 at breakfast/dinner and 3 at lunch (eat a small lunch) and still no burning etc. which means I still need to work my way up! Also & sorry if TMI but there is no undigested food in my stool which I didn't even realize I had really until this!! I think I have been eating low carb and taking supplements for the past 3 years but my body hasn't received anywhere near that nutrition from poor acid!! Alot of digestive problems plague my mom's side of the family.

After I get my labs I want to see where my FBG is though...it was high last time and has been a concern. I really need to use that meter I bought but I dread poking myself LOL I think I might need to try the cycloset too I just don't know if my Dr. will be on board with it. We have a lot of the same issues Gretchen!

> **Destiny;43320 wrote:** NG, how much of progesterone do you take? I take about 50 mg a day and it is not doing its job.

Just this past cycle I went up to .1ml (10 mg) for 10 days after my period & then .2ml (20 mg) until my period begins. But funny thing is I always had 28 day cycles before I started PG now my cycle is 25-26 days???? I just don't get it??? And my periods are still very slow starting... the first few days just a little and then it works its way up. Used to just BAM be there! LOL But that started happening before the PG...

### nonchalant — 2012-09-05T01:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-hormones.2854/post-49478>

Well, in the last couple weeks I've started pregnenolone and DHEA, but I really think it was the seafood and CT. Earlier this year, when I was heavy into CT, I thought I might be starting, based on feelings down there. But nothing happened. I started with the daily seafood just before the 30 day seafood challenge, and I started back up with CT three weeks ago. I've been respecting light cycles all of this year.

I guess it's hard pinning down the exact cause. I still haven't gotten any of the fancy micronized pregnenolone, I was waiting for the summer heat to end before ordering the type Jansz suggests. And the DHEA was just some that I picked up at the grocery store. I didn't feel much of an effect from either.

Like the doc says, though, everyone is different. I've been working hard at getting my hsCRP number down.

### Grizz — 2012-08-04T19:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-ll.2856/post-42863>

Somehow the original iodine thread got closed by accident, an Youthfuliz could not post her valuable story. Here it is:

> Just a quick outline:
>
>
>
> July 4th: found a lump on my neck while looking in the mirror. Felt it...about the size of a small egg, moved up and down when I swallowed.
>
>
>
> July 5th: went to the health center, got blood test and ultrasound done.
>
>
>
> July 10th: results came back: blood test showed normal thyroid hormones. Ultrasound showed two nodules, size 2.9 and 2.1. Most likely benign but biopsy was suggested due to the size.
>
>
>
> Between July 5th and July 10th: I read online and found that Iodine deficiency is the main cause for thyroid nodules. I read the iodine threads on mda and jackkruse.com (I also post on those forums using the same id), asked questions there (thanks Grizz and others who replied) and started to supplement iodine from Saturday July 7th. I also ate a lot of seaweeds and oysters starting that weekend. I couldn't find Lugol's iodine or iodoral locally so I used potassium Iodide that my local vitaminshoppe carried for nuclear emergency... I was using about 50mg a day. The nodules reacted almost immediately to the iodide... the lump was obviously smaller right away, felt softer too, and became about half the original size within a few days. I ordered iodoral and lugol's online and when my order arrived I had just finished the bottle of potassium iodine so I continued to take iodoral 50mg daily.
>
>
>
> July 12: saw a doctor who again suggested biopsy. The only reason for the biopsy was the size of the nodules. He was also against iodine supplementation. But by then the lump has shrunk so much I knew I'm better off sticking to iodine. I also read online the potential problems with biopsy so I decided not to have the biopsy. Instead I'll take iodine for a few months and repeat the ultrasound to see how much the nodules have shrunk.
>
>
>
> Since then I have been taking 50mg iodoral daily with all the cofactors. There was a day or two I took more than 50mg, and there were days when I added lugol's to my water... I don't think I ever did more than 75mg in any day. Very mild detox, if any, mostly in the forms of stinky sweat, slight headache occasionally, and loose bowels occasionally. The benefits include increased energy, feeling more motivated, and oh I got so much done lately nothing seems difficult any more.
>
>
>
> The lump was not noticeable since a few weeks ago, and now is hardly palpable. I still see a small lump moving when I swallow but it's so much smaller than before... maybe less than 1/3 the size now.
>
>
>
> So this is my one month iodine supplementation summary. Will update again in another month.
>
>
>
> I'm taking 75mg today (37.5 at breakfast, 37.5 later).
>
> [http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1970777#i](http://http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1970777#i)

Doctors reports, iodine research, testimonials, procedures and more here:

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

Grizz

NWgirl,

Sorry for the delay in answering your message. We just got back from a 9 day Bus Tour of the Western States. Great fun!

Any Vitamin C will probably work. The Iodine Doctors recommend Glycinate because it is most easily absorbed and causes the least amount of indigestion problems. If your Ascorbic Acid does not upset your stomach, it is probably OK.

I have just updated the list of required supplements to show the range of dosage, and the reasons for taking a higher dosage.

\- - - NOTE ON DOSAGES: The range of doses are intended for the amount of Detox problems people have. Smaller dosage is for those with minimal detox symptoms. Large dosage is for people with severe detox symptoms or for those who cannot absorb iodine. Always work with your doctor when taking supplements.

\- - Â½ tsp Natural Celtic Salt, Himalayan Pink Salt, Redmonds Salt or Hawaiin black salt

\- - - If the sea salt is pure white, it is unacceptable (minerals have been stripped out )

\- - 200 to 400mcg selenium (L-selenomethionine preferred)

\- - 400 mg Magnesium - Glycinate preferred

\- - 2,000 to 5000mg Vitamin C - Ester-C preferred - do not take with iodine

\- - ATP Cofactors caplet contains both 100mg B2 & 500mg B3 non-flushing in the correct ratio. Take 1/2 caplet with iodine up to 25mg, then one caplet for each 50 mg iodine,

May I ask for more details on your problems and your recovery since starting iodine supplements?

Our neighbor recently told us how thrilled she was taking iodine with required supplements. Her doctor had told her to learn to live with her painful swollen breasts or else cut them off. She went home crying. Amazingly, her pain was gone within a few days of starting iodine, and her lumps & bumps were GONE in only 1 month. She is now eager to help me present a seminar on iodine at our local church. We plan to fill all 500 seats by word of mouth and email notifications. I'm also planning to write magazine articles on this subject to get the word out. 100 million women are severely suffering from iodine deficiency and the subject of iodine is buried, whitewashed, and downplayed.

Latest Doctors Reports, iodine research, testimonials, procedures and more here:

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

NWgirl,

What are your symptoms? Bloat, pimples & headaches are almost always caused by taking too much iodine too soon, which causes more toxins to come out of our cells than our liver & kidneys can handle. Here is the recommended schedule:

\- - - Mix 1 drop 2% Lugols into many bottles of water, put the caps back on.

\- - - Week 1 drink 1 oz (~30ml) daily (about 380mcg iodone)

\- - - Week 2 drink 2 oz (~60ml) daily (about 775mcg iodine)

\- - - Week 3 drink 4 oz (~120ml) daily ( about 1.5mg iodine)

\- - - Week 4 drink 6 oz (~180ml) daily ( about 2.3mg iodine)

\- - - Week 5 drink 8 oz (~220ml) daily ( about 3.1 mg iodine or about 1 drop per day),

\- - - Week 6 drink 2 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 7 drink 3 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 8 drink 4 drops per day (about 12.5mg = to 1 iodoral)

\* Next, Titrate up with iodoral 12.5mg iodine tablets available at Amazon.Com

\- - - add 1/2 tablet each month to eventually reach 25mg daily-refer to Dr. Brownstein’s Book

\- - - 25-50mg Daily is the dosage target recommended by Dr. Brownstein to overcome toxins

\- - - 6 to 50mg Daily is his latest recommendation for maintence (07/27/12)

\* If any detox symptoms appear do the salt flush and pulse dose. (all listed below)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-20T01:36:04+0300

[http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

### chocolate — 2012-08-20T06:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-ll.2856/post-45816>

[http://ithyroid.com/copper.htm](http://http://ithyroid.com/copper.htm)

> In a study on rats there is evidence that copper is necessary for proper iodine metabolism and consequently of proper thyroid hormone synthesis.
>
>
>
> Title \[The effect of copper on the metabolism of iodine, carbohydrates and proteins in rats\]
>
>
>
> Author
>
> Esipenko BE; Marsakova NV
>
> Source
>
> Fiziol Zh, 36(2):35-43 1990 Mar-Apr
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Experiments on 156 rats maintained at ration with copper deficiency have demonstrated a decrease in the values of iodine metabolism in organs and tissues excluding the liver where a sharp increase in the concentration and content of inorganic iodine was observed. A disturbance in indices of carbohydrate and proteins metabolism in the organism of animals is marked. A direct relationship with a correlation coefficient equaling 0.87-1.00 is determined between changes in the concentration of protein-bound iodine in blood and concentration of glycogen in the liver, skeletal muscles, albumins, alpha 1-, alpha 2-globulins, urea concentration; an inverse relationship with glucose, activity of blood lipo-dehydrogenase and liver mitochondria, aldolase, concentration of pyruvic and lactic acids is established as well. It is concluded that copper deficiency can exert both a direct effect on metabolic processes (as data from literature testify) and an indirect one disturbing iodine metabolism, i. e. sharply decreasing protein-bound iodine production by the thyroid gland.

**

Nonchalant,

You are detoxing with **too much** iodine as proven by your symptoms. The toxins being released by iodine are too much to be handled by the liver & kidneys so your skin is erupting. It doesn't matter whether the iodine is in seafood or in Lugols, you are too full of toxins for the iodine that you are taking. STOP and scale WAY back on your iodine consumption.

Substantially Reduce your iodine intake (including seafood) and do the pulse dose 2 times a week and be sure you are taking all of the required co-supplements which are required to get the toxins out of your body.

The following are recommended:

\- - Â½ tsp Natural Celtic Salt, Himalayan Pink Salt, Redmonds Salt or Hawaiin black salt

\- - - If the sea salt is pure white, it is unacceptable (minerals have been stripped out )

\- - 200 to 400mcg selenium (L-selenomethionine preferred)

\- - 400 mg Magnesium - Glycinate preferred

\- - 2,000 to 5000mg Vitamin C - Ester-C preferred - do not take with iodine

\- - ATP Cofactors caplet contains both 100mg B2 & 500mg B3 non-flushing in the correct ratio. Take 1/2 caplet with iodine up to 25mg, then one caplet for each 50 mg iodine,

Start VERY SMALL** with your iodine so your liver & kidneys can keep up with the toxins:

( whether seafood or Lugols)

\- - - Mix 1 drop 2% Lugols into many bottles of water, put the caps back on.

\- - - Week 1 drink 1 oz (~30ml) daily (about 380mcg iodone)

\- - - Week 2 drink 2 oz (~60ml) daily (about 775mcg iodine)

\- - - Week 3 drink 4 oz (~120ml) daily ( about 1.5mg iodine)

\- - - Week 4 drink 6 oz (~180ml) daily ( about 2.3mg iodine)

\- - - Week 5 drink 8 oz (~220ml) daily ( about 3.1 mg iodine or about 1 drop per day),

\- - - Week 6 drink 2 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 7 drink 3 drops per day in a glass of water

\- - - Week 8 drink 4 drops per day (about 12.5mg = to 1 iodoral)

\* Next, Titrate up with iodoral 12.5mg iodine tablets available at Amazon.Com

\- - - add 1/2 tablet each month to eventually reach 25mg daily-refer to Dr. Brownstein’s Book

\- - - 25-50mg Daily is the dosage target recommended by Dr. Brownstein to overcome toxins

\- - - 6 to 50mg Daily is his latest recommendation for maintence (07/27/12)

\* If any detox symptoms appear do the salt flush and pulse dose. (all listed below)

Complete details here:

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T15:11:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awsome-results-achieved-in-5-10-months.2862/post-43033>

I am posting this for my husband who was a participant in this new lifestyle with me. He was not active participant on the website but got a lot of its info through me.

Started LRx - the end of November 2011

Started CT - twice a day since the mid of February 2012

Starting weight - 229 lbs, 6'2"

Health issues - Crohn's disease, high BP, arthritis pain, elevated blood sugar, frequent sinusitis, allergies - was taking Claritin and Afrin all year round, acne

He also had a brain fog, tired - in his words - I felt like I was slowly dying ......

**Results:**

Lost 43 lbs - presently at 186 lbs

Crohn's - no problems - might be gone?

Normal BP

Normal blood sugar

Allergies gone - taking nothing

Arthritis - gone

Clear skin

Feeling sharp and healthy

Now, he is going to have all the recommended labs done to see if any additional tweaking is needed.

Hey Soul,

When you say "ferment the heck out of it," how long do you let your first ferment go? I started homemade kombucha about a month ago by growing my own scoby from a bottle of plain kombucha. After about 14 days I ended up with a nice, super thick scoby, but it seems to take forever to ferment a batch of tea. My current batch has been fermenting for 12 days and still tastes pretty sweet to me. Granted, I keep my house at a fairly cool 68 degrees, but I didn't expect it to take that long.

The kids and I devour this stuff like crazy and I can't afford store bought anymore, so I think I'm going to have to have 2-3 gallons going at a time!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T00:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45449>

> **BJK77;45044 wrote:** Hey Soul,
>
>
>
> When you say "ferment the heck out of it," how long do you let your first ferment go? I started homemade kombucha about a month ago by growing my own scoby from a bottle of plain kombucha. After about 14 days I ended up with a nice, super thick scoby, but it seems to take forever to ferment a batch of tea. My current batch has been fermenting for 12 days and still tastes pretty sweet to me. Granted, I keep my house at a fairly cool 68 degrees, but I didn't expect it to take that long.
>
>
>
> The kids and I devour this stuff like crazy and I can't afford store bought anymore, so I think I'm going to have to have 2-3 gallons going at a time!

Sorry BJ I never saw this. I ferment my kombucha until it hits ph 2.8 - 3.5 with test sticks. Anything below 2.5 is vinegar if I recall correctly... but then I love vinegar  I try to get close to 3.5 or under but not over, and then I do a second ferment in the bottled state to allow it to become fizzy. The first fermentation can take forever based on the room temp, the lower the temp the longer it will take. 68 is pretty low for kombucha. Probably 3 weeks and counting at that temp.

I have 5 gallons going at a time..... my kitchen is a lab  Last scobies were taking up half the gallon jar, they were HUGE. Then the fruit flies got them.... oh well. I got more in the fridge.

I also go by taste. There is a degree of tart that is too tart.... undrinkable, vinegar. And I don't like the sweet stuff either. I think your problem might be the room temp. Try some plant starter mats or reptile warming mats (wrap the container or put it under) or find a warmer spot in your house (higher up will be warmer, my fermentation vessels are on top of my pantries in the kitchen right under the ceiling).

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T00:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45452>

**Anna K.;44853 wrote:** Ok, Kombucha does has some caffeine in it if you use normal caffeinated tea to make it, but the amount is so insignificant that MOST people don't notice that.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

According to one site the amount of caffeine in kombucha is similar to decaf coffee:

[http://www.katalystkombucha.com/faq.html](http://http://www.katalystkombucha.com/faq.html)

Q: Is there any caffeine in Kombucha?

A: We have calculated that our Kombucha has approximately 5 mg of caffeine per serving. This as opposed to other caffeinated beverages is the lowest we have encountered , and those with sensitivity to caffeine have reported no adverse effects from our Kombucha.

Caffeine Content in 8-ounce Beverage: (in milligrams)

Coffee, Drip--115-175

Tea, instant--30

Tea, green--15

Hot cocoa--14

Coffee, Decaf, brewed--3-4

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

another place reports this:

"caffeine present in Kombucha is paired with a natural amino acid that slows itâ€™s absorption into the bloodstream, l-theanine, with great side benefits."

[http://www.kombuchakamp.com/2011/03/kombucha-and-caffeine-explained.html](http://http://www.kombuchakamp.com/2011/03/kombucha-and-caffeine-explained.html)

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Looks like the culture eats some of the caffeine from the tea and the longer you culture it, the less caffeine is left and what is left in it is diluted in a much larger quantity of liquid then a cup of tea.

"the amount of caffeine in the finished product is about 1/2 of what it was when you started with the plain tea. We use a range of teas, Black being the highest, green next, white, and rooibos which has no caffeine and has to be combined with a black or green. The SCOBY "eats" the caffeine and converts it. Without caffeine it cannot live. So, if you took an 8oz cup of black tea which has 40mg of caffeine (less than a 1/3 cup of coffee) and used it to make kombucha you would yeild 20mg of caffeine. That's a couple sips of coffee."

[http://nessalla.com/node/11](http://http://nessalla.com/node/11)

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Also, many people dont' recommend using caffeine-free tea for it because bacteria needs it. This is the best link about the use of different teas for it:

[http://www.happyherbalist.com/differentteas.aspx](http://http://www.happyherbalist.com/differentteas.aspx)

How to Decaffeinate Kombucha Tea

Kombucha Tea= about 1/3 of the typical caffeine of a cup of tea

Many kombucha authors and brewers debate whether Kombucha needs caffeine or not. In our research and in at least one journal the bacteria are energized by caffeine so some caffeine would seem wise. The typical amount of tea per batch of Kombucha Tea is 1/2 oz per 3 quarts (5-7 t-bags/ 15 grams). Strong tea (steeping a long time or using more tea ) provides more sterols and tannins and generally benefit the yeasts.

If you are sensitive to caffeine and prefer not to use herbal (non-caffeine\_ teas than we suggest to simply use less tea. (use 1/3 less) or Pre-Steep for 30 seconds in boiling water (removes 80% of the caffeine). Discard that water. Steep tea for less than 5 minutes.

Kombucha can survive on the above method. But this will make a very weak tasting Kombucha Tea. You may notice that you can also now ferment for a few days longer decreasing the sugar content as well as increasing the perceived health benefits form a longer brew cycle. A longer ferment will also reduce the caffeine as the caffeine gets cannibalized as the other food sources are depleted. Unfortunately longer ferments also produce a very sour kombucha tea. If that happens try mixing with sparkling mineral water and enjoy a pleasant Kombucha Tea without as much caffeine or sugar.

This is \*a\* site that says this there is other studies that show that the caffeine is almost unaffected by the fermentation. Most websites that sell you kombucha or supplies will try to keep this issue hidden. There is definitely caffeine in kombucha..... I live in the Republic of Kombucha drinkers and we all agree.... there is caffeine in it. I'm one of the best test individuals for caffeine... there is caffeine in it.

There is a reason why people are giving up their morning joe for kombucha... b/c it has caffeine. Why they want it as a pick me up.... b/c it has caffeine.

Anyways. Do with it what you want or run your own experiment. I still believe my own body first.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T01:03:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45454>

> **freshveggies;44832 wrote:** I don't like the caffeine either---what kind of tea do you use for your Kombucha?

I used to use black and green mix. That's the most traditional version. But b/c I'm so sensitive to the caffeine I've done a whole lot of experiments from decaf to roiboos to herbal. Thing is, you want to expose the mushroom to caffeine every few ferments to keep it nice and healthy. If you can't have the caffeine at all, make some friends happy  Or barter, that's what I do  Decaf and roiboos work both, herbal you have to be careful b/c some will damage the culture but it takes longer in general for the fermentation to happen. You could increase room temp to speed it up. In the summer it works no problem in the winter it can be a bit of a pain.

### freshveggies — 2012-08-18T02:20:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45469>

> **Souldanzer;45825 wrote:** I used to use black and green mix. That's the most traditional version. But b/c I'm so sensitive to the caffeine I've done a whole lot of experiments from decaf to roiboos to herbal. Thing is, you want to expose the mushroom to caffeine every few ferments to keep it nice and healthy. If you can't have the caffeine at all, make some friends happy  Or barter, that's what I do  Decaf and roiboos work both, herbal you have to be careful b/c some will damage the culture but it takes longer in general for the fermentation to happen. You could increase room temp to speed it up. In the summer it works no problem in the winter it can be a bit of a pain.

thanks for the tips. I am hoping to start this fall. Going to ferment coconut water first.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-20T19:31:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45871>

Thanks Soul! It's finally starting to get more tart and surprisingly fizzy already too even though it's just covered loosely with a towel. I love the fizz, so no complaints here  I think I'll get a warming mat for the next batch though to see if that helps speed it along.

5 gallons brewing? I'm in awe! I think I'm going to have to check into a continuous brewing system or just make sure to have several gallons going at once. The whole family is obsessed with this stuff and I love that it's an easy way to get some probees in them. So much easier than water kefir too, which I always seem to kill somehow!

> **Souldanzer;45820 wrote:** Sorry BJ I never saw this. I ferment my kombucha until it hits ph 2.8 - 3.5 with test sticks. Anything below 2.5 is vinegar if I recall correctly... but then I love vinegar  I try to get close to 3.5 or under but not over, and then I do a second ferment in the bottled state to allow it to become fizzy. The first fermentation can take forever based on the room temp, the lower the temp the longer it will take. 68 is pretty low for kombucha. Probably 3 weeks and counting at that temp.
>
>
>
> I have 5 gallons going at a time..... my kitchen is a lab  Last scobies were taking up half the gallon jar, they were HUGE. Then the fruit flies got them.... oh well. I got more in the fridge.
>
>
>
> I also go by taste. There is a degree of tart that is too tart.... undrinkable, vinegar. And I don't like the sweet stuff either. I think your problem might be the room temp. Try some plant starter mats or reptile warming mats (wrap the container or put it under) or find a warmer spot in your house (higher up will be warmer, my fermentation vessels are on top of my pantries in the kitchen right under the ceiling).

### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-20T21:38:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-45904>

New here and LOVE LOVE all this talk about fermented foods!

I get my stuff from my local farmer's market. We use young coconut keifer ( I put in my smoothies) and fermented veggies. We have dropped a few lbs, flatter tummies, too!

### diane — 2012-08-21T02:58:12+0300

the Body Ecology lady (Donna Gates) uses it a lot.

### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-21T16:22:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-46032>

What is Kevita? I see it's a probiotic drink.

Can it be consumed other ways, like in a smoothie? I love "sneaking" healthy things in my smoothies.

### Pamela — 2012-08-25T04:57:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-46952>

I've been brewing Kombucha for a few months now and have gotten my procedure down pretty good. Right now I am only brewing one gallon at a time. The first scoby I bought was from someone on ebay and it was a very small scoby that didn't really produce how I wanted it to. Then I went to [kombuchakamp.com/](http://http://www.kombuchakamp.com/) and bought a very nice full size thick scoby, it was worth the price. I have also purchased my tea from them and the kombucha turns out very tasty every time. I've played around with the amount of sugar, 1C or 1.25C doesn't seem to matter much which I use and I brew for one full week in the summer, then do a 2nd ferment for 2 days. In the 2nd ferment I bottle into 3 bottles (old CO glass bottles) and add cut up ginger to them for the 2 days. At the end of the 2 days I then strain and pour the tea into old KT Kombucha glass bottles and then refrigerate. It is really great stuff and the cost is so much less.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-25T22:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/post-47117>

> **diane;46343 wrote:** How do you ferment coconut water? I can't believe how much I'm learning here!

You can buy the frozen young coconut water (used to be called body ecology but now is branded diff but same company) at some specialty health food stores. You can also buy the coconuts and tap into them for the water. I've done both in the past. Now I am just using filtered water, some starter, and a little bit of fresh fruit in the jar and two days later I have fizzy, non-sweet, water. Really good. Plus I still make the Kevita water, which I guess this is like that too but it is not made with probiotics.

What starter do you use?

# Thread: CT and post-op pain!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobH — 2012-08-05T23:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/post-43118>

After fighting an infected (and thankfully benign -- the infection occurred after a biopsy!) abdominal wall mass thought to be scar tissue from a prior surgery for nearly a month, I had surgery for it last Tuesday night.

The whole story is pretty bizarre, and I'm still recovering from a month of serious systemic inflammation (HS-CRP was 77 immediately pre-operatively 8^(.

But what I am here to say is wow! CT works for post-op pain -- went into the operation pretty well cold-adapted (have been doing full-body ice baths at least 3 times / week all summer, and sometimes 4-5 times / week. Even post-operatively I can't do immersion yet (drains in place) but have been doing a lot of spot icing of the surgical area.

Post-op pain has been nearly nonexistent -- NOTHING like I remember from the original operation (liposxn / abdominoplasty) that probably caused this scar tissue in the first place. I've taken nearly nothing for pain control since I left the hospital (1 pill -- my wife tricked me... 8^)) and feel great every time I ice the area. Drains are now out and I can't wait to be able to jump back into full-immersion (ok, I don't use a snorkel). It just doesn't hurt. And that is with about 24 inches of scar, a big T-shaped incision with a whole bunch of staples, and a whole lot of dissection and retracting necessary to get the mass out (according to the surgeons).

A long way to climb back towards optimal but I can literally feel the systemic difference now that the inflamed and infected tissue is out of me, and feeling so much better everyday.

I want to thank Jack for helping make the decision to have the operation (I knew it but he made sure I had no alternative... 8^)) and for all of the amazing work he has done and continues to do.... I'm trying to paleo-it-forward.

RobH

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-05T23:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-post-op-pain.2868/post-43121>

Rob you found out what I did when I did my bio hack.......it is amazing for surgical cases......

### ealachan — 2012-08-07T03:17:30+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-07T03:32:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunrise-photos.2875/post-43340>

ok I just signed up and I still can do it? How to you get it here from photobucket?

Attached files [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/43627=399-IMG_0829.JPG)

### ealachan — 2012-08-07T03:47:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunrise-photos.2875/post-43342>

> **Jack Kruse;43627 wrote:** ok I just signed up and I still can do it? How to you get it here from photobucket?

Once you've uploaded the picture, if you look off to the right it should show you a little box that says "Links" and has 4 different types of code: email / IM, direct link, HTML and IMG. If you click on the code next to IMG, it should copy it for you automatically. Then you come back here and paste that code into the body of your message, and your picture should show up when you post.

You can also get the line of code directly from the album page once you've uploaded. Under each picture thumbnail, there will be the same little boxes of code. You want the IMG one.

Hope that helps!

### NWgirl — 2012-08-06T16:35:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-43199>

Ok sorry I'm reducing the forum to poop talk. I've had diarrhea the whole time on leptin rs, it has been about 5 weeks. Really doing great except for the diarrhea. This morning I woke up and with a BM came a lot of bright blood, Hemorrhoids!! Holy Hemorrhoids, what should I do now? Any natural cures out there or should I visit a doc?

### ealachan — 2012-08-06T16:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-43201>

I dunno what to tell you about the hemorrhoids themselves, but re: the diahrrea, I found that taking a digestive enzyme before meals has helped MASSIVELY. I was basically running to the bathroom 20 minutes after every meal for a while there...turned out that because I don't have a gallbladder, my body was unable to break down all the fat I was eating and was just having a dump reaction every time. A couple days on the enzymes cleared it right up. I've been using the Rainbow Light brand in conjunction with an ox bile supplement and my toilet visits have since improved tremendously.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-43219>

Shitty situation.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-06T18:29:20+0300

> **Jack Kruse;43506 wrote:** This means a gut flora issue (Diarrhea) and it means a high omega six (hemorrhoids)

So I guess only time on the diet will help lower omega 6 levels correct? I do eat about 2-3 T of coconut oil per day. I don't eat ANY almonds and only eat a brazil nut or two occasionally but haven't in about 5 days.

The diarrhea is major for me. It's what kept me abandoning a paleoish diet multiple times.

I have made homemade sauerkraut many times but ran out so I guess some good probiotics are in order plus some probiotic foods.

Thank goodness I have a 30 min consult with jack soon. I'm just waiting for my hormone panel to come back.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-06T18:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-43233>

> **NWgirl;43518 wrote:** So I guess only time on the diet will help lower omega 6 levels correct? I do eat about 2-3 T of coconut oil per day. I don't eat ANY almonds and only eat a brazil nut or two occasionally but haven't in about 5 days.
>

>
> The diarrhea is major for me. It's what kept me abandoning a paleoish diet multiple times.
>
>
>
> I have made homemade sauerkraut many times but ran out so I guess some good probiotics are in order plus some probiotic foods.
>
>
>
> Thank goodness I have a 30 min consult with jack soon. I'm just waiting for my hormone panel to come back.

Do lots of probiotic foods, and the bone broths are great for helping the gut - once you start having it on hand then you realize all the things you can slip it in. Basically any cooking liquid can be a bone broth. Also, the non-probiotic veggies are great at feeding the good bacteria in your gut, so try and eat as much of the non-starchys and a variety of them. In case you didn't get the memo, more seafood and sea-veggies!

### chocolate — 2012-08-12T00:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-hemorrhoids.2877/post-44415>

[http://glutenfreeworks.com/blog/2010/07/28/understanding-copper-deficiency-in-celiac-disease/](http://http://glutenfreeworks.com/blog/2010/07/28/understanding-copper-deficiency-in-celiac-disease/)

I've seen on several websites that folks with bypass operations have trouble with copper deficiency.

> Copper deficiency is characterized by fatigue, anemia, neutropenia (low level of neutrophils, the most common type of white blood cell that protect against infection), leukopenia (abnormal decrease of leukocytes or white blood cells), bone and joint abnormalities, skin abnormalities, impairment of nerve and muscle function, impairment of adrenal and thyroid gland function, reproductive difficulties and loss of hair and skin color. In children, neutropenia and leukopenia are the best early indications of copper deficiency. **Changes that are due to defective connective tissue formation include bleeding into the covering of bones, bleeding from aneurysms and bleeding from hemorrhoids.** Death may result from brain degeneration, failure to make blood cells and hemorhage.

### catzarewonderful — 2024-10-10T23:19:07+0300

Should i stop also eating nuts? Organic nuts are a go to snack for me.

I'm early 40s, i don't want an operation to get rid of them. I know i can do this myself, but I'm out of ideas.

Supplements, daily,
Cod & skate liver oil
Creatine
Clorite dioxide

Please help!
